1.session_b_note: Contrition originates in the interaction between God and the human inner being — specifically in the inner being's encounter with its own moral condition before a holy God. The crushing vocabulary (dkh/ktt/nkh roots) is passive: the contrite person is the one who has been crushed, broken, brought low. The origin is not primarily a human decision but a condition produced by divine-human encounter — divine holiness exposes human unworthiness, and the inner being that receives this is crushed. However, the refusal of contrition (Jer 44:10) implies it can be resisted, which means the human will is implicated even if the origin is relational. Source observations: OBS-030-007, OBS-030-045, OBS-030-060, OBS-030-062.

Forgiveness originates in God's own character — it is a divine attribute before it is a divine act. The Hebrew sal.lach (Psa 86:5) and se.li.chah (Psa 130:4) name it as a possessed quality belonging to God's essential nature. The Greek corpus extends this: Eph 1:7 ("according to the riches of his grace") grounds forgiveness in grace as the upstream source-disposition. The structural origin is therefore: (1) dispositional — forgiveness resides in God's character as an intrinsic quality; (2) covenantal — it is expressed through covenant structures (cultic OT; new covenant NT); (3) atonement-grounded — in the NT, the blood of Christ is the mechanism that makes the disposition actionable for sinners. Human forgiveness is structurally derivative: it originates in having received the divine forgiveness that transforms the inner being (Luk 7:47). (OBS-064-019; OBS-064-022; OBS-064-003)

Goodness originates in God — this is stated explicitly in the registry description (OBS-001) and confirmed by the verse evidence. Psa 119:68 (OBS-026) models the being/doing relationship at the divine level: God is good and does good. Gen 1:31 (OBS-036) establishes goodness as the ontological baseline — creation is declared very good by its maker. Human goodness is structurally derivative: a participation in and reflection of divine goodness. Mic 6:8 (OBS-027, OBS-028) confirms this — "He has told you, O man, what is good" positions the definition of goodness as divinely revealed, not humanly generated.

2.session_b_note: For contrition (unlike grace or mercy, which are extended), contrition is a human posture, not a divine gift extended to humans. What determines its presence is the human inner being's orientation before God: whether it is lowly/humbled or hardened/resistant. Isa 66:2 identifies humility and trembling at God's word as companions of contrition — these mark the inner-being posture that produces the contrite condition. Jer 44:10 identifies the failure to fear, walk in God's law, and humble oneself as producing the hardened alternative. Source observations: OBS-030-047, OBS-030-060, OBS-030-062.

Divine side: God's own character is the basis for forgiveness being available ("to all who call upon you" — Psa 86:5). The conditions under which God forgives are: intercession/prayer (5379-002 corpus, Dan 9:19); repentance and faith (NT corpus); covenantal ritual in OT (5379-001 corpus). God withholds forgiveness where the condition is permanently violated (Mar 3:29 — blasphemy against the Spirit). Human side: commanded forgiveness is extended when the offender repents (Luk 17:3-4); the spirit of forgiveness is commanded as a regular disposition (Mar 11:25). Forgiveness is withheld when the heart-level release is absent (Mat 18:35 — "from your heart"). The withholding by humans has consequences: the unforgiving person's own forgiveness is jeopardised (Mat 6:14-15). (OBS-064-024; OBS-064-031; OBS-064-016)

The tension between the registry-level dimension label (Moral/Conscience) and the group-level dimension spread (Moral Character 05, Cognition 03, Volition 04, Divine-Human Correspondence 11, and three NULL groups) is real and analytically significant. The registry-level label is a coarse aggregate that does not capture the genuine plurality of inner-being phenomena H2896A addresses. This finding supports the answer to Q002 (partial coverage): for goodness, a single registry-level dimension label is inadequate — the word operates across at least four confirmed dimensions. The label 'Moral/Conscience' captures the dominant human register (884-002, 884-006) but leaves the experiential (884-003), comparative-evaluative (884-004), volitional (884-008), and ontological (884-007) registers unnamed. The appropriate action is a dimension review that assigns group-level dimensions to the three NULL groups and re-evaluates 884-003 and 884-005.

3.session_b_note: Three modes identified from Stage 2a — (1) Penitential-relational mode: the spirit/heart is crushed by its own moral condition before God; this is the Dependence/Creatureliness mode (groups 7552-003, 7553-001, 7557-001, 7554-001, 7555-001). (2) Experiential-anguish mode: the inner being is crushed by suffering, adversity, or divine chastisement; this is the Emotion — Negative mode (groups 7552-001, 7554-002, 7556-001). (3) Substitutionary mode: the Servant's inner being is crushed by divine will on behalf of others, producing peace and healing for the community; this is the Divine-Human Correspondence mode (group 7552-002). Source: OBS-030-023, OBS-030-025, OBS-030-026, OBS-030-041, Q&A 004.

Four distinct modes: (1) Divine covenantal-objective — OT cultic forgiveness: sacrifice + atonement → forgiveness declared without named inner-being prerequisites. (2) Divine relational-personal — NT: Jesus pronounces forgiveness in personal encounters (Luk 7:47-48, Mat 9:2); faith/repentance named as access-postures. (3) Human horizontal — commanded release of the inner claim against an offender "from your heart" (Mat 18:35); modelled on received divine forgiveness. (4) Intercessory — seeking forgiveness on behalf of others (Dan 9:19, Num 14:19, Moses; 5379-002 corpus). These four modes are not isolated — the intercessory mode assumes the covenantal-objective; the human horizontal mode assumes the relational-personal received. (OBS-064-014; OBS-064-029; OBS-064-031; OBS-064-016)

Nine distinct modes identified from the H2896A group structure (OBS-048 — DIM-67-001 resolution), plus two Greek terms adding further modes:
(1) Doxological assertion — God's character declared good (884-001)
(2) Moral character — human inner quality of goodness (884-002)
(3) Experiential orientation — nearness to God as the highest experiential good (884-003)
(4) Comparative evaluation — goodness as the ranking operator in wisdom's better-than sayings (884-004)
(5) Covenantal word — God's promise and faithfulness as good (884-005)
(6) Moral verdict — recognising what is not good; internalised moral failure (884-006)
(7) Creative pronouncement — the Creator's evaluative declaration of creation as good (884-007)
(8) Volitional preference — what is good in the eyes of / what one chooses or yields to (884-008)
(9) Inner well-being state — the shalom-condition of gladness and well-being (884-009)
(10) Spirit-produced disposition — goodness as pneumatologically enabled inner quality (885-001, G0019)
(11) Divine generous goodwill — God's kindness as inner disposition toward humanity (886-001, G5544)
(12) Spirit-produced kindness — the human expression of chrēstotēs (886-002, G5544)

Analytical assessment (per prior anchor verse work):
- 884-007 (creation pronouncement, Gen 1:31): Dimension 11 — Divine-Human Correspondence is the closest available fit, but the group is distinctive — it is a divine creative-evaluative act that establishes ontological goodness. If the dimension review introduces a 'Divine Agency' or 'Creation/Ontology' dimension, this group would fit better. Provisional: Dimension 11.
- 884-008 (volitional preference idiom, 40 verses, anchor Jer 26:14): Dimension 04 — Volition. The group description explicitly names choosing, preferring, agreeing, judging fitting. This is squarely volitional.
- 884-009 (inner well-being state, 5 verses, anchor Est 5:9): Dimension uncertain — neither Moral Character (05), Cognition (03), nor Volition (04) fits cleanly. The group names an affective inner state (tov-lev, glad of heart). Closest: a Vitality/Experiential dimension not currently in the active vocabulary. Flag for dimension review.

**GAP QUESTION — GAP-N-001:**
> For registries where verse context groups carry affective inner-being states (gladness, well-being, shalom-condition) that do not fit Moral Character, Cognition, or Volition, what dimension should be assigned? The current 10-dimension vocabulary may require an Experiential/Affective category for these groups.

DIM-67-001 asked whether the wide dimensional range of tov represents genuinely distinct inner-being phenomena or a unified category. Resolution: the modes are genuinely distinct — each of the nine H2896A groups engages a different inner-being faculty (divine doxological assertion, human moral character, experiential orientation, evaluative cognition, covenantal reception, moral verdict, ontological pronouncement, volitional preference, affective well-being). They are unified not lexically but theologically: goodness originates in God and all modes express, derive from, or respond to the one goodness that is God's own. The Hebrew tov covers this unified-yet-plural field with a single form; English requires multiple words to render the plurality.

DIM-67-001 used older dimension labels: 'Theological/Divine-Human', 'Moral/Conscience', 'Spiritual/God-ward', 'Character/Disposition'. Against current vocabulary:
- 'Theological/Divine-Human' → Dimension 11 — Divine-Human Correspondence
- 'Moral/Conscience' → Dimension 05 — Moral Character
- 'Spiritual/God-ward' → closest is Dimension 05 (Moral Character) or Dimension 08 (Dependence/Creatureliness) depending on group
- 'Character/Disposition' → Dimension 05 (Moral Character) for human; Dimension 11 for divine

The re-reading confirms: DIM-67-001's substance is valid and the analytical resolution (genuinely distinct modes unified theologically) holds under current vocabulary. The finding's conclusion is unchanged; only the vocabulary labels require updating in the DB record.

4.session_b_note: In the penitential-relational mode, contrition produces readiness to receive divine revival. Isa 57:15 states the divine response to the contrite spirit is "revival" of spirit and heart. Psa 34:18 indicates divine nearness and salvation as the outcome. Psa 51:17 indicates divine acceptance of the contrite heart as the inner sacrifice. In the substitutionary mode (Isa 53:5), the Servant's crushing produces peace and healing in the community. In the experiential-anguish mode, contrition produces suffering/dismay without a specified transformative outcome in the verse evidence itself. Source: OBS-030-046, OBS-030-048, OBS-030-052, OBS-030-053, OBS-030-041.

Two named downstream inner states: (1) Love (agapē) — Luk 7:47: forgiveness received generates love-orientation in a quantity-proportional relationship. (2) Reverential fear — Psa 130:4: the presence of divine forgiveness (as a known quality) produces reverence. Implied downstream states: relief from guilt (group 879-001 — "released from guilt and restored to right standing"), enabling of worship (Mat 5:24), enabling of direct knowledge of God (Jer 31:34). The primary named production is love (Luk 7:47), with reverential fear named in Psa 130:4 as the response to encountering forgiveness-as-divine-quality rather than forgiveness-personally-received. (OBS-064-020; OBS-064-021; OBS-064-029)

Goodness produces several inner-being states in the recipient depending on the mode:
- Encounter with divine goodness produces trust and refuge-taking (Psa 34:8, OBS-025) and receptive learning (Psa 119:68, OBS-026).
- The experiential good of nearness to God produces a stable inner orientation — the person makes God their refuge and finds what truly satisfies (Psa 73:28, OBS-029).
- Reception of God's good covenantal word produces inner knowing in heart and soul — a verified trust (Jos 23:14, OBS-033).
- The Spirit-produced quality of goodness fills the person and is completed by God (885-001 group description; Gal 5:22, OBS-042).
- The recognition of one's own not-goodness produces self-loathing and moral revulsion (Eze 36:31, OBS-035).

5.session_b_note: The verse evidence for contrition does not directly address how the contrite person relates to other humans. The data is predominantly about the vertical relationship (human before God). Psa 51 (the penitential psalm context) does mention sins against Bathsheba/Uriah implicitly, but the verses in the registry do not address horizontal relational outcomes of contrition. The substitutionary group (Isa 53:5) describes a change in the community's relational position toward God (peace produced), but this is the outcome of the Servant's crushing, not of the community's contrition. Source: OBS-030-041, OBS-030-052.

The relational position shift is: from debtor/offender to released person (vertical, before God) and from offended/withholder to forgiver (horizontal, toward others). Specifically: (1) Restored relational standing before God — sin broke the relationship; forgiveness restores it (group 5377-001 corpus). (2) The obligation/capacity to forgive others — Luk 7:47-48 shows love as the relational posture toward Jesus that forgiveness produces; Mat 18:35 shows that forgiveness received creates the structural obligation to extend forgiveness. (3) Enabling of reconciliation — Mat 5:24. The relational position change is from claim-holder to claim-releaser, and from debtor-standing to restored-standing. (OBS-064-029; OBS-064-032; OBS-064-028)

Mic 6:8 (OBS-027) names the relational expression of goodness as threefold: doing justice (toward others), loving kindness (toward others), walking humbly with God (vertical relation). The good person's inner character expresses itself in right conduct toward others. Col 3:12 (OBS-046 context) names kindness as a garment the renewed person puts on — a relational disposition toward the community. The comparative wisdom group (884-004) ranks inner relational qualities (humility, patience, kindness) above external achievement, implying the good person's relational orientation is characterised by these inner qualities. The Spirit-produced goodness of 885-001 is expressed in "righteous conduct and resolve" (group description).

6.session_b_note: God's disposition toward the contrite human being is one of nearness, attention, and revivifying action. Psa 34:18: "The Lord is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit." Isa 57:15: God dwells with the contrite spirit "to revive the spirit of the lowly, and to revive the heart of the contrite." Isa 66:2: God looks specifically at the humble, contrite, and trembling. Psa 51:17: God will not despise the broken and contrite heart. The pattern is consistent: divine nearness, attention, salvation, and life-giving response to the contrite inner being. Source: OBS-030-045, OBS-030-046, OBS-030-048, OBS-030-052, OBS-030-053, OBS-030-062.

God's disposition toward the human is revealed as: (1) Inherently forgiving — sal.lach (Psa 86:5) names readiness-to-forgive as an intrinsic character attribute, not a reactive decision. (2) Reliably available — "to all who call upon you" (Psa 86:5) universalises the access. (3) Abundant — "abounding in steadfast love" frames the forgiveness disposition in generosity. (4) Motivationally pure — "for your own sake" (Dan 9:19) grounds forgiveness in God's own character rather than human merit. (5) Relationally generative — forgiveness is not merely juridical but produces inner transformation (Luk 7:47). Together these reveal that God's disposition toward the human is one of generous readiness to release legitimate claims against the sinner — not reluctant, not capricious, but constitutively and abundantly forgiving. (OBS-064-019; OBS-064-016; OBS-064-029)

The divine goodness groups reveal God's disposition comprehensively:
- 884-001: God's character is fundamentally good — his steadfast love, name, and Spirit are all described as good. His goodness governs his relationship to all he has made (Psa 34:8, Psa 145:9).
- 884-005: God's disposition toward his people is expressed in faithful promise-keeping — his good word does not fail (Jos 23:14, OBS-033).
- 884-007: God's disposition at creation is one of generous evaluative affirmation — he sees what he has made and declares it very good (Gen 1:31, OBS-036).
- 886-001: God's chrēstotēs (kindness) is a generous goodwill toward humanity — it leads to repentance (Rom 2:4) and is displayed supremely in Christ (Eph 2:7). It operates in a relational field alongside severity (Rom 11:22, OBS-043).

7.session_b_note: God's nearness to the contrite is grounded in God's own character, not in the merit of the contrite person. Isa 57:15 frames the divine promise with divine self-identification (the high and holy One, inhabiting eternity) — the basis is who God is, not what the person has done. The contrite person has done nothing to earn divine nearness; the contrite posture is precisely the absence of claims to merit (brokenness, lowliness). Isa 66:2 parallels this: God "looks" to the humble and contrite — the divine gaze is sovereign, not obligated. Source: OBS-030-045, OBS-030-062.

God's forgiveness is established on: (1) His own character — sal.lach as intrinsic attribute (Psa 86:5). (2) His covenant — the cultic structure (Lev 4:20), the new covenant declaration (Jer 31:34). (3) His grace — "according to the riches of his grace" (Eph 1:7). (4) The atoning blood of Christ — "through his blood" (Eph 1:7), "without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness" (Heb 9:22). The basis is not human merit but divine character expressed through covenantal structure and atoning mechanism. The forgiveness basis is therefore theologically layered: character → covenant → atonement → reception. (OBS-064-019; OBS-064-022; OBS-064-014)

8.session_b_note: The somatic loci for contrition in the human recipient are "spirit" (ruach) and "heart" (lev), consistently across multiple anchor verses. Psa 34:18: "crushed in spirit" and "brokenhearted." Isa 57:15: "contrite and lowly spirit" and "revive the spirit / revive the heart." Psa 51:17: "broken spirit" and "broken and contrite heart." Isa 66:2: "humble and contrite in spirit." Spirit and heart are the two primary inner-being loci, often appearing together as a bicolon or pairing. For the Servant (Isa 53:5), the somatic expression is bodily — pierced, crushed, wounded — but the outcomes (peace, healing) are relational/inner-being. Source: OBS-030-045, OBS-030-048, OBS-030-052, OBS-030-060.

The somatic location of forgiveness is indirect in this registry. In the giver (God/Christ): the blood of Christ is the somatic mechanism of forgiveness (Eph 1:7; Heb 9:22) — forgiveness is costly at the somatic level for the Forgiver. In the human giver: the heart (*kardia*, Mat 18:35) is named as the inner locus from which genuine forgiveness must come — "from your heart." In the recipient: the downstream somatic expression appears in the woman of Luk 7:47 (weeping, kissing, anointing) as bodily evidence of inner transformation. Relationally: forgiveness locates itself in the vertical human-God relationship (guilt released, standing restored) and the horizontal human-human relationship (claim released, relational space restored). The primary relational location is the debt-claim — forgiveness operates precisely where a legitimate claim exists. (OBS-064-030; OBS-064-031; OBS-064-022)

- In the giver (God): Goodness is located in God's character, name, steadfast love, and Spirit (884-001 group description). It is not located in a specific somatic organ. The Spirit of God (Neh 9:20, Psa 143:10) is called "good" — goodness is attributed to the divine Spirit as the operative agent.
- In the human recipient: Jos 23:14 (OBS-033) explicitly locates the reception of covenantal goodness in the "hearts and souls" — the inner being as the site of knowing. Est 5:9 (OBS-040) locates the inner well-being state in the "heart" (tov-lev — glad of heart). The experiential good of 884-003 is located in the inner reorientation of the person (Psa 73:28).
- Relationally: Mic 6:8 locates goodness in three relational practices (justice, kindness, humility with God) — outward-relational and vertical expressions.

9.session_b_note: The verse evidence presents contrition as a settled inner-being condition (an ongoing posture) rather than a discrete act. The adjectival forms (H1793A dak.ka "contrite," H5223 na.kheh "contrite/smitten") and the participial uses of H1792/H1794 describe a person who is characterised by this condition. Isa 66:2 identifies the person as one who habitually trembles at God's word — an ongoing orientation, not a single episode. However, the group descriptions include both the state (the contrite spirit) and the process (the crushing that produces it), suggesting the state has a history of formation. Source: OBS-030-009, OBS-030-021, OBS-030-060.

Both. The one-time act: the cultic atonement (Lev 4:20) and the pronouncement "your sins are forgiven" (Luk 7:47-48) — specific discrete events. The ongoing condition: (1) Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more" — the new covenant form involves a permanent cognitive state-change (the debt is not merely cancelled at a point in time but rendered permanently non-recallable). (2) Luk 17:3-4 — repeated forgiveness commanded (up to seven times a day) — the human act is ongoing and renewable, not a single event. (3) The Lord's Prayer (Mat 6:12; Luk 11:4) — daily forgiveness-of-debts as an ongoing practice. The distinguishing criterion: the divine forensic act of pardoning is often a discrete event (pronounced, complete); the ongoing condition is the relational state it establishes and the practice it generates. (OBS-064-017; OBS-064-031)

G0019 (agathōsunē, 4 verses — all relevant) and G5544 (chrēstotēs, 7 verses — all relevant) are thin-corpus terms. Their evidence supports analytically plausible conclusions but the evidential weight is proportionally limited — no single-verse conclusion about these terms can be treated as programme-confirmed without acknowledgment of this limitation. The thin-evidence designation applies to the Greek component of this registry's analysis. All conclusions derived from G0019 and G5544 are held as analytically supported but not definitively confirmed until a larger corpus would validate them. The Hebrew H2896A (306 verses) provides the primary evidential weight for this registry.

10.session_b_note: The spatial language is consistently downward and inward: "crushed to the ground" (Psa 143:3), "of a lowly spirit" (Isa 57:15 — the Hebrew shefel/humble implies low elevation), "broken" (shabar — structural collapse). The direction of contrition is downward and inward — a lowering of the inner being that removes self-elevation. The divine response language is then upward: "to revive" (restore life/elevation). The spatial logic: contrition moves inward and downward; divine response moves outward and upward. Source: OBS-030-038, OBS-030-045, OBS-030-046.

The corpus uses some directional language. "Send away/release" is the root act-image of aphiēmi — a releasing motion, a sending away of the claim. Luk 4:18 uses afesis in the phrase "release to captives" — a liberation-from-captivity image (directional: out of captivity, into freedom). Act 26:18 describes the conversion sequence as "from darkness to light, from the power of Satan to God" — a directional movement in which forgiveness is a stage. Jer 31:34 implies directional movement: the burden of iniquity is lifted (though the verb is not directional). The dominant spatial image is release rather than movement — the claim is released, the burden removed, the captive freed. (OBS-064-017)

11.session_b_note: Contrition itself does not produce enabling capacity; it produces the conditions for divine enabling. The enabling comes through the divine response to contrition: reviving the spirit (Isa 57:15), divine nearness and salvation (Psa 34:18). The contrite state is not itself a capacity — it is a condition of lowliness that makes the person receptive. The enabling capacity is received from outside (divine revival) rather than generated from within. Source: OBS-030-046, OBS-030-048.

Yes. Multiple enabling capacities named: (1) Capacity to love — Luk 7:47 (forgiveness generates love-orientation). (2) Capacity to worship — Mat 5:24 (reconciliation/forgiveness enables the offering to be made). (3) Capacity to know God directly — Jer 31:34 (forgiveness removes the barrier to direct knowledge). (4) Capacity to live relationally without the weight of unresolved guilt — group 879-001 corpus. (5) Implied: capacity to extend forgiveness to others — the structural congruence of Mat 18:35 implies that those forgiven are enabled (not merely required) to forgive. The enabling is primarily relational and cognitive rather than volitional or somatic. (OBS-064-029; OBS-064-028; OBS-064-017)

12.session_b_note: Contrition as a settled condition produces the quality of lowliness/humility — it is paired with "lowly" in Isa 57:15 and with "humble" in Isa 66:2. The character quality produced is not righteousness or moral achievement but a posture of lowliness before God — the absence of self-claim. This is consistent across the Dependence/Creatureliness groups. The contrition vocabulary does not produce pride, self-assertion, or claims of merit; it produces the opposite. Source: OBS-030-045, OBS-030-060, OBS-030-061.

The corpus names love (agapē) as the primary character quality produced (Luk 7:47). Reverential fear/awe is named as a second quality (Psa 130:4). The structural congruence principle (Mat 18:35) implies that receiving forgiveness produces a forgiving character — the one who has been forgiven is being formed into one who forgives. This is not merely an obligation but an inner-being formation: the experience of forgiveness reshapes the relational disposition of the inner being toward others. The character quality is therefore: generous relational disposition toward others, patterned on having been forgiven. (OBS-064-029; OBS-064-020; OBS-064-032)

Goodness in its Spirit-produced form (885-001, 886-002) generates:
- Goodness as a character quality in itself (agathōsunē — virtue/beneficence, Rom 15:14)
- Kindness as character quality (chrēstotēs — Col 3:12: put on kindness as garment of the renewed person)
- The capacity for honest moral self-assessment — 884-006 and Eze 36:31 show that genuine engagement with goodness/not-goodness produces the character quality of moral self-awareness and honest self-evaluation
- Integrity — 884-002 includes verses where "good" names the upright person who walks with integrity (Pro 19:1; Pro 28:6)
The registry description (OBS-001) frames this derivatively: the character quality of goodness in the human is a participation in divine goodness, not an independently generated virtue.

13.session_b_note: The verse evidence is sparse on horizontal relational expression of contrition. The data is predominantly vertical (human before God). The only corporate instance (Psa 44:19 — Group 7554-002) involves corporate suffering rather than interpersonal expression of contrition. The penitential Psalm 51 context implies contrition arising from wrongs done to others, but the verses in the registry focus on the posture before God rather than the relational repair of horizontal relationships. Source: OBS-030-056, OBS-030-057, Q&A 010.

Horizontally: the act of releasing others from the inner claim (Mat 18:35 — "forgive your brother from your heart"; Luk 17:3-4 — repeated forgiveness of the repentant). Through prayer: intercession for forgiveness on behalf of others (Dan 9:19, Num 14:19, Amos 7:2 — 5379-002 corpus). Through reconciliation: Mat 5:24 (leaving the offering to be reconciled first). The relational expression requires the heart-level release (Mat 18:35) — outer statement without inner release is insufficient. The expression is also corporate: Luk 11:4 (Lord's Prayer — "forgive us" as community prayer practice). The corporate dimension includes intercession for the community (Solomon's prayer, 1Ki 8; Daniel's community intercession, Dan 9:19). (OBS-064-031; OBS-064-016; OBS-064-028)

Multiple relational expressions visible:
- Justice toward others (Mic 6:8, OBS-027) — goodness expressed as right action in the human community
- Kindness toward others (chrēstotēs — Col 3:12; Gal 5:22) — the relational disposition of generous goodwill
- Beneficence/generosity (agathōsunē — Gal 5:22 glossed as "generosity," OBS-008) — goodness expressed as outward-facing generosity
- Instructing and building up others (Rom 15:14 — "full of goodness... able to instruct one another")
- The "good news" motif (Pro 25:25, Isa 52:7) — goodness communicated as life-giving word to others
- The not-good register: 884-006 names failure in relational conduct (partiality, violence, false scales) as expressions of not-good

14.session_b_note: The ground of genuine contrition is honest self-knowledge before God — specifically, the recognition that one has actually caused harm or broken trust (per registry description: "not the surface shame of being caught but the interior grief of having actually caused harm or broken trust"). Isa 66:2 identifies humility (lowliness) and trembling at God's word as the orientations accompanying genuine contrition. Psa 51:17 distinguishes genuine inner sacrifice (broken spirit, contrite heart) from ritual sacrifice — genuine contrition comes from the inner being, not from external compliance. Source: OBS-030-001, OBS-030-052, OBS-030-053, OBS-030-060, OBS-030-061.

The heart is named as the required locus: Mat 18:35 — forgiveness must come "from your heart." This distinguishes genuine from performative forgiveness. Beyond the heart-locus, the structural ground identified in the corpus: (1) Having received divine forgiveness — the parable of Mat 18:35 implies that genuine human forgiveness is grounded in recognising the magnitude of what one has received. (2) The love-orientation produced by forgiveness received (Luk 7:47) — the inner orientation of love is itself the ground from which horizontal forgiveness flows. (3) The prayer context (Mar 11:25) — forgiveness extended in the posture of prayer connects horizontal forgiveness to the vertical relationship. The ground is therefore: heart-level release, rooted in received forgiveness, expressed in love-orientation toward others. (OBS-064-031; OBS-064-029; OBS-064-032)

15.session_b_note: A three-stage sequence is discernible from the verse evidence: (1) Crushing/breaking — the inner being is broken down (through moral self-knowledge, divine chastisement, or suffering); (2) Low/contrite posture — the broken inner being adopts a posture of lowliness before God (spirit and heart humbled); (3) Divine response — God draws near, revives, saves, accepts. The sequence is: crushing → contrite posture → divine response. Psa 51:8 adds a further movement: the bones broken by God (stage 1–2) are invited to rejoice (stage 3+), suggesting revival follows contrition. Source: OBS-030-046, OBS-030-048, OBS-030-052, OBS-030-055.

All three temporal orientations present. Past: "her sins, which are many, are forgiven" (Luk 7:47 — declared forgiven, completed act). "Your sins are forgiven you" (group 5377-001 corpus, multiple pronouncement contexts). Present: "with you there is forgiveness" (Psa 130:4 — ongoing present possession); Luk 17:3-4 (ongoing daily practice of extending forgiveness). Future: Jer 31:34 — new covenant declaration: "I will forgive their iniquity" (future tense, prophetic). Heb 9:22, 10:18 — the forward-looking dimension of OT atonement (anticipating the blood that enables genuine forgiveness). The past dimension (completed forgiveness) is the most common in the NT pronouncement corpus. The present dimension dominates the divine-attribute corpus (Psa 86:5, Psa 130:4). The future dimension is primarily prophetic (Jer 31:34). (OBS-064-017)

Yes — two sequences are visible:
(1) Divine goodness → encounter → trust/refuge → testimony (Psa 34:8, OBS-025; Psa 73:28, OBS-029): God is good → person tastes/perceives → takes refuge → declares God's works. The sequence moves from divine attribute through inner perception to relational response.
(2) Recognition of not-good → remembering → self-loathing → repentance (Eze 36:31, OBS-035): the person remembers their not-good ways → internalises the verdict → loathes themselves → the sequence implies moral transformation. This is the negative sequence that leads to the positive.
(3) Spirit-filling → goodness as character quality → expression in conduct (885-001, OBS-042): Spirit produces goodness → goodness fills the inner being → expressed in resolve and righteous conduct.
The sequences are not uniform across all twelve modes — they are characteristic of specific groups.

16.session_b_note: The direction is inward and downward at the stage of contrition (crushing, lowering, breaking down); then outward and upward through divine response (revival, nearness, salvation). The word does not remain in the downward register permanently — the verse evidence consistently presents the contrite state as the threshold for divine reviving movement. The sequence of direction: down (crushing) → low (contrite posture) → up (revival). Source: Q&A 015, OBS-030-015 (spatial language — Q&A 015).

Both. Individual: the majority of the NT pronouncement corpus names individuals ("your sins are forgiven" — Luk 7:47-48, Mat 9:2, Luk 5:20). Community: the OT intercessory corpus (5379-002) includes corporate intercession — Moses for Israel (Num 14:19), Daniel for the nation (Dan 9:19), Amos for Jacob (Amos 7:2), Solomon's prayer for national scenarios (1Ki 8). The Lord's Prayer (Luk 11:4 — "forgive us") frames forgiveness as a community prayer. Act 2:38 — community-level proclamation ("each of you"). The cultic OT corpus includes both individual (Lev 4:26 — individual offering) and community forgiveness (Num 15:25-26 — "all the congregation of Israel shall be forgiven"). The scope is comprehensively both individual and communal. (OBS-064-016)

17.session_b_note: Contrition's originating source in the verse evidence is the encounter with God and with one's own moral condition before a holy God. The vocabulary does not primarily identify a human decision to be contrite — it identifies a condition produced by encounter. Isa 66:2 presents contrition as God-determined ("this is the one to whom I will look") — God identifies the contrite; God does not produce them by fiat, but the encounter with the holy God (Isa 57:15 — the high and holy One) is the context from which the contrite posture emerges. In Psa 51, the context is explicit: divine confrontation through prophetic word (Nathan) that breaks the king's defences and produces contrition. Source: OBS-030-001, OBS-030-006 (Q001 answer), OBS-030-062.

The corpus does not directly address developmental growth in the capacity to forgive. However, two indirect observations: (1) Luk 7:47 implies that the larger the forgiveness received and internalised, the larger the love-orientation generated — suggesting a proportional growth mechanism (more received → more produced). (2) Luk 17:3-4 commands repeated forgiveness (seven times a day) — the practice of repeated forgiveness may develop the inner capacity, though this is implied rather than stated. The command in Luk 17:3-4 assumes that forgiveness of repeated offenders is achievable — implying the inner capacity is sufficient for the demand. Whether this grows over time is not directly addressed. (OBS-064-029)

18.session_b_note: The verse evidence does not directly address the origin of the human capacity to seek contrition. The passages present contrition as arising in encounter rather than as sought deliberately. The closest the data comes is the three-fold posture of Isa 66:2 (humble + contrite + trembling at word) — these together suggest a person oriented toward God's word, from which contrition emerges. Whether this orientation is itself a divine gift or a human capacity is not addressed in the registry's verse evidence. Source: OBS-030-060, OBS-030-062.

Yes. Receiving side: the woman of Luk 7:47 expresses forgiveness received through bodily acts (weeping, anointing) — these are evidence of a felt inner transformation. Psa 130:4 — reverence produced by knowing forgiveness is available (a felt disposition). The Psalmist's cry "O Lord, forgive!" (Psa 25:11; Dan 9:19) is emotionally charged — urgency and longing are present. Giving side: Mat 18:35 — the requirement for heart-level release implies that the act is felt, not merely cognitive or verbal. The parable implies that the master's compassion (splagchnizomai, Mat 18:27 — visceral compassion) was the felt precondition of his forgiving act. (OBS-064-030; OBS-064-031; OBS-064-020)

19.session_b_note: The primary roots (dkh, ktt, nkh) are shared across the contrition vocabulary internally. The nkh root (H5222, H5223) is shared with "wound" vocabulary (mak.kah — H4347) and "to smite" (na.khah — H5221) — these are not inner-being registries in the programme as far as can be determined from the available data. The dkh root family (H1790–H1796) is self-contained within the contrition vocabulary. No confirmed shared etymology with another inner-being characteristic registry is identifiable from the available data. The related term H7533 ra.tsats (XREF from R105 lust) shares crushing vocabulary but a different root. Source: OBS-030-019, OBS-030-020, OBS-030-005.

Yes — verbal/performative forgiveness without heart-level release. Mat 18:35 implies this by requiring forgiveness "from your heart" — the contrast is with forgiveness that is declared or assumed without the inner-being release. The parable implies that the servant's receipt of forgiveness was not actually internalised (he did not genuinely appropriate it at the inner level), which is why he could immediately turn to throttle his fellow servant. The counterfeit is: outer acknowledgment of having been forgiven without the inner-being transformation that genuine reception would produce. The outer religious conduct vs inner abandonment of love (Rev 2:4 — Ephesian church) represents a similar form: maintaining the form of relationship without the inner disposition. (OBS-064-031; OBS-064-027)

Yes — extensively. The TOV root family (OBS-007) is distributed across three registries:
- Registry 67 (this registry): H2896A (tov, adjectival — pleasant/good) — goodness as quality
- Registry 65 (generosity): G0018 (agathos) and the agatho- compound family (doing good, to do good) — goodness as active doing/generosity
- Registry 103 (love): H2895 (tov verbal — be pleasing), H2898 (tuv — goodness, nominal) — goodness as relational pleasantness/affection
The Greek OWNER terms (G0019 agathōsunē, G5544 chrēstotēs) each have related-word families (OBS-007, OBS-010): agathōsunē shares root with agathos (G0018) and all agatho- compounds; chrēstotēs shares root with chrēstos (G5543, good/kind) and chrēsteuomai (G5541, be kind) and chrēstologia (G5542, smooth talk — the distortion form).

20.session_b_note: This question, framed around "receiving" the word, implies contrition is something received and transmitted (like grace or forgiveness). In R030's frame, contrition is not typically "received" by one person from another — it is the condition of the person before God. However, reading the question as "what does the experience of contrition produce as a subsequent inner response": the verse evidence suggests the movement from contrition toward longing for joy and gladness (Psa 51:8 — "let me hear joy and gladness; let the bones that you have broken rejoice"). The natural inner movement after contrition is toward hoped-for restoration. Source: OBS-030-055, Q&A 015.

Freedom and choice operate on both sides. Divine side: forgiveness is God's sovereign choice — he chooses to forgive (Jer 5:7 — "how can I pardon you?" suggests a question God must resolve; Lam 3:42 — "you have not forgiven" — God can choose not to forgive). Human side — receiving: forgiveness is offered but must be accessed (faith, repentance, confession) — these are volitional postures. Human side — extending: forgiveness is commanded but its genuineness requires heart-level engagement (Mat 18:35). The command structure implies freedom: "forgive your brother" (Luk 17:3-4) — it can be done or refused, but refusal has consequences. The freedom dimension is most explicit in the parable of Mat 18:35: the servant freely chose not to forgive, and that choice had consequences for his own forgiven state. (OBS-064-024; OBS-064-031; OBS-064-032)

21.session_b_note: The structural opposite of contrition is pride — the inner-being posture of self-elevation that refuses lowliness before God. Isa 57:15 presents the high and holy God dwelling with the contrite and lowly; the implicit alternative is the proud who do not receive divine dwelling. Jer 44:10 presents the hardened, unhumbled posture as the explicit alternative within the registry's verse evidence. The inner-being logic of the opposite: self-sufficiency, refusal of divine assessment, closure to God's word. Source: OBS-030-028, OBS-030-034, OBS-030-047, Q&A 026.

The impacts named in the corpus: (1) The woman of Luk 7:47 — weeping, bodily devotion, love expressed somatically — complete inner transformation visible in behaviour. (2) Love generated (Luk 7:47) — relational orientation toward the forgiver/Forgiver. (3) Reverential fear/awe (Psa 130:4) — not the response of presumption but of awed recognition. (4) Restored relationship — "your sins are forgiven you" (Luk 7:48) — the recipient is released and restored. (5) The paralytic's physical healing follows forgiveness in Mat 9:2-6 — suggesting the inner release of forgiveness can manifest in bodily restoration. The visible impact is primarily inner-to-outer: the inner transformation produces visible love, devotion, and life-change. (OBS-064-029; OBS-064-030; OBS-064-020)

The presence/absence/distortion structure confirmed across Registry 67:
- Presence: Groups 884-001 through 884-005, 884-007 through 884-009 (H2896A positive forms); 885-001, 886-001, 886-002 (Greek positive forms)
- Absence/Negation: 884-006 (not-good prophetic/wisdom verdict, 22 verses) for H2896A; 886-002/Rom 3:12 ('no one does good, not even one') for G5544 — the universal absence of chrēstotēs diagnoses the fallen human condition
- Distortion: Not present as a distinct verse-context group. Etymologically embedded in G5542 (chrēstologia — smooth talk) as a related word. Behaviourally present in Mal 2:17 (calling evil good) and Jer 44:17 (redefining good as material advantage) within Group 884-006 — but these are in the not-good verdict group, not a separate distortion group.
Conclusion: the tripartite model holds structurally for R67; the distortion pole is etymological rather than textual in the active verse corpus.

22.session_b_note: Merit-logic blocks contrition. Contrition arises from the recognition of unworthiness — the opposite of a merit-claim. Psa 51:17 explicitly displaces the sacrificial system (a merit-exchange logic) with the inner sacrifice of contrition: "you will not delight in sacrifice... the sacrifices of God are a broken spirit." The merit-claiming person — who approaches God with ritual performance as currency — has not arrived at the posture of brokenness that contrition requires. Merit-logic closes the inner being to contrition; contrition is precisely the abandonment of merit-claims. Source: OBS-030-052, OBS-030-053, OBS-030-054.

Guilt — the unresolved debt state. Mar 3:29 — "guilty of an eternal sin" — permanent, unresolvable guilt. Mat 18:35 — the restored debt (the entire cancelled debt reinstated when forgiveness is refused). Mat 6:15 — withholding forgiveness → one's own forgiveness withheld. The absence of forgiveness produces: (1) Accumulated guilt (the prior state that forgiveness addresses — 73 guilt registry, 51 shared verses). (2) Relational closure — the debt-claim that separates. (3) The inner-life poisoning identified in the registry description — the withholder is damaged by their own withholding. (The description names this; the strongest verse evidence is Mat 18:35 showing consequences for the unforgiving servant.) (OBS-064-024; OBS-064-032)

23.session_b_note: The verse evidence does not directly address whether contrition is "extended" to others in the way that grace or forgiveness is. Contrition is primarily a condition one inhabits, not something one extends. However, the substitutionary group (Isa 53:5 — the Servant's crushing) does involve one person's contrition-vocabulary experience producing inner-being outcomes for others (peace, healing). In this vicarious register, the Servant's willingness to be crushed without merit-claim produces benefit for the merit-lacking community. Source: OBS-030-041, OBS-030-042.

The corpus suggests some context-dependence. Intercessory context (5379-002): forgiveness sought for a community under judgment — the context is national sin and covenantal consequence (Daniel, Moses, Amos). Cultic context (5379-001): forgiveness through ritual — applies specifically in the temple/sacrifice context, not available outside that structure. New covenant context (5379-003, 879-001): universally available through faith in Christ, not structure-dependent. Horizontal context (5377-002, G0863H group): operates in interpersonal relationships, requiring heart-level engagement and ongoing practice. The operate-differently question is partially answered: forgiveness operates through different mechanisms in different covenantal contexts (OT cultic vs NT relational), and with different subjects (divine vs human agency). (OBS-064-014; OBS-064-006; OBS-064-017)

24.session_b_note: Forgiveness reveals: (1) The human inner being accumulates guilt — it registers wrongs and holds them as legitimate claims. Forgiveness addresses a real inner-being phenomenon (guilt). (2) The inner being is capable of genuine relational release — Mat 18:35 commands heart-level forgiveness, implying the inner being has this capacity. (3) The inner being is transformed by received forgiveness — Luk 7:47 shows forgiveness can reorient the entire inner disposition of love. (4) The inner being can hold claims at the heart level even while appearing to have released them — Mat 18:35 distinguishes verbal from heart-level forgiveness. (5) The inner being is damaged by unforgiven claims — the description notes that withholding forgiveness poisons the inner life of the withholder. Forgiveness therefore reveals: the human inner being is a claim-registering, guilt-holding, relationship-orienting reality that can be both transformed by received forgiveness and damaged by forgiveness withheld. (OBS-064-001; OBS-064-031; OBS-064-029)

Two forms of non-recognition are present:
(1) The Haman instance (Est 5:9, OBS-040): inner well-being (tov-lev — glad of heart) that is not grounded in genuine goodness — a person experiencing a form of the good (honour, pleasure) but whose inner well-being is fragile, morally ungrounded, and instantly disrupted. Non-recognition here is the condition of experiencing a surface good without perceiving its source or testing its depth.
(2) The not-good group (884-006): the person who walks in ways that are not good (Isa 65:2 — "a rebellious people who walk in a way that is not good, following their own devices") is in the condition of having departed from goodness while apparently unaware of the verdict. Jer 6:16 names this directly: the people are offered "the good way" but refuse it ("we will not walk in it").

25.session_b_note: Forgiveness reveals God as: (1) Intrinsically forgiving — sal.lach as character attribute (Psa 86:5), not a reactive decision. (2) Abundantly forgiving — "abounding in steadfast love" frames the disposition in generosity. (3) Covenantally committed — the new covenant promise (Jer 31:34) is unconditional and permanent. (4) The only one who can forgive at the deepest level — H5545 (sa.lach) has exclusively divine subject; "who can forgive sins but God alone?" (Mar 2:7). (5) Relationally generative — his forgiveness produces inner transformation (love, reverence) in the recipient. (6) Responsive to intercession — the 5379-002 corpus shows God responding to persistent plea. (7) Just in forgiveness — "faithful and just to forgive" (1Jo 1:9) — forgiveness is grounded in justice, not in setting justice aside. (OBS-064-003; OBS-064-019; OBS-064-016)

Two cultivating experiences are visible:
(1) Encounter with divine goodness — Psa 34:8 (OBS-025): tasting that the Lord is good produces the inner state that positions one for trust and relational openness. The person whose inner being has been oriented toward divine goodness (Psa 73:28) naturally overflows toward others (Rom 15:14: "full of goodness... able to instruct one another").
(2) Spirit-filling — the Spirit-produced goodness of 885-001 and 886-002 is the enabling source. Gal 5:22 presents chrēstotēs and agathōsunē as Spirit-fruit — the inner experience of Spirit-filling produces the capacity to express goodness toward others. Col 3:12 frames this as "putting on" kindness — a deliberate inner act enabled by the Spirit's prior work.

26.session_b_note: Earnest appeal (lament/petition before God) in the contrition vocabulary expresses the posture of acknowledged need and unworthiness. Psa 143:3 is a lament — the psalmist is crushed and appeals to God from that position. Psa 51:8 is a petition arising from the broken state ("let me hear joy and gladness"). The act of appeal from a crushed/contrite position expresses: (a) recognition that the one appealed to has the power to revive; (b) recognition that the appealer has no resources of their own; (c) openness of the inner being to receive what God gives. Source: OBS-030-038, OBS-030-055.

Yes — both in receiving and withholding. Community receiving: OT intercessory corpus (Num 15:25-26 — "all the congregation shall be forgiven"; Daniel's corporate intercession; Solomon's prayer for national scenarios). Lord's Prayer ("forgive us" — corporate prayer practice). Act 2:38 — community baptism for forgiveness. Withholding community effect: Mat 6:14-15 implies a community dynamic — unforgiveness within a community blocks the community's relationship with God. The relational congruence principle (Mat 18:35) applies to interpersonal community dynamics — a community in which people refuse to forgive each other is one in which the structural coherence of the forgiven-forgiving relationship is broken. (OBS-064-032; OBS-064-016)

27.session_b_note: The somatic forms of seeking (contrition as an inner movement toward God) are: groaning/groaning aloud (Psa 38:8 — "I am feeble and crushed; I groan because of the tumult of my heart"), the broken spirit offered in prayer/worship (Psa 51:17 — the broken spirit is the offering itself), trembling (Isa 66:2 — trembles at my word, a somatic expression of the inner condition). The somatic expressions are: voicing (groan), offering (bringing the broken inner being to God), and physical trembling. Source: OBS-030-055, OBS-030-052, OBS-030-060.

Yes. The strongest evidence: Luk 7:47 — the woman's entire inner orientation of love is named as the lasting transformation produced by forgiveness received. Jer 31:34 — forgiveness as the basis of a new covenantal condition (permanent, not periodic — "I will remember their sin no more"). The "remember no more" clause names an enduring cognitive-relational change rather than a renewable act. The love-orientation produced by forgiveness (Luk 7:47) is not a momentary emotional state but a relational disposition toward Jesus that characterises the woman's inner being. The restoration of relational standing (group 5377-001 corpus — "your sins are forgiven") implies an enduring new status rather than a temporary relief. (OBS-064-017; OBS-064-029)

28.session_b_note: The inner-being orientation that closes off contrition is hardness/resistance — specifically: (a) failure to humble oneself (Jer 44:10 — they have not humbled themselves); (b) failure to fear (Jer 44:10 — nor have they feared); (c) failure to walk in God's word (Jer 44:10 — nor walked in my law). These three constitute the closed posture. The inner-being closure is the refusal of lowliness in the presence of the holy God. Source: OBS-030-028, OBS-030-047, Q&A 026.

NT consistently names access-postures: (1) Repentance — Mar 1:4, Luk 24:47, Act 2:38, Act 5:31. (2) Faith — Act 10:43 ("everyone who believes receives forgiveness"). (3) Confession — 1Jo 1:9 ("if we confess our sins"). (4) Prayer/intercession — the 5379-002 corpus throughout. In the OT cultic corpus (5379-001), the posture is participation in the prescribed ritual — no specific inner-being posture named beyond the ritual act. In the new covenant (5379-003, Jer 31:34), the posture is less specified — it is received as part of the covenant people. The posture question is nuanced: these are access-postures, not merit-conditions. They name the inner orientation through which forgiveness is received, not the basis on which it is earned. (OBS-064-014; OBS-064-006)

Three closing orientations are named:
(1) Self-direction — Isa 65:2: "a rebellious people who walk in a way that is not good, following their own devices." Following one's own devices is the inner orientation that closes off the good way.
(2) Refusal of the good way — Jer 6:16: explicitly named. The people are offered "the good way" and refuse it ("we will not walk in it"). The will's refusal is the closing mechanism.
(3) Moral self-justification in wrong orientation — Jer 44:17 (884-006 notes): the people recall false prosperity in idolatry as "good" — the inner orientation that redefines what is good according to its own advantage closes off recognition of genuine goodness.
(4) Unbelief / departure — Rom 11:22 (OBS-043): "otherwise you too will be cut off" — departure from faith/trust is named as the condition that closes off the ongoing experience of divine kindness.

29.session_b_note: In the OT cultic mode: the prescribed sacrifice is the prerequisite (Lev 4:20 — the right offering through the right priestly mediation). In the NT: prior conditions include repentance and faith (access-postures as named above in Q028). For human horizontal forgiveness: the offender's repentance appears in Luk 17:3-4 as a condition ("if he repents, forgive him"), though the pattern in Mat 18:35 suggests the heart-posture of the forgiver is the primary preparation. The preparation on the divine side: the atonement of Christ is the cosmic preparation that makes NT forgiveness possible (Eph 1:7, Heb 9:22). (OBS-064-014; OBS-064-022)

30.session_b_note: Contrition and weakness are structurally aligned in the verse evidence. Psa 38:8 — "I am feeble and crushed" — explicitly pairs weakness and contrition (H1794 crushed). Psa 34:18 — the "brokenhearted" and "crushed in spirit" are in a condition of weakness (they require saving). The contrite person is, by definition, in a condition of inner weakness — they cannot revive themselves. This is the theological significance of the divine response: the weak/crushed inner being draws divine strength/revival. Contrition is therefore not a strength but a posture of disclosed weakness that attracts divine action. Source: OBS-030-048, OBS-030-055.

Resistance sources in the corpus: (1) Religious authority misused: Mar 2:7 — "who can forgive sins but God alone?" — scribes resist Jesus' act of forgiveness on theological grounds. (2) Structural incoherence: the servant of Mat 18:35 who resists extending forgiveness despite having received it. (3) Volitional refusal: Mat 6:15 implies a person who chooses not to forgive. (4) Pharisaic obstruction: Mat 23:13 (while in the permit-meaning, the same aphiēmi vocabulary) — those who shut access rather than open it. The source of resistance is primarily: volitional (chosen refusal), religious (misunderstanding divine prerogative), and structural-incoherence (failure to receive at the heart level what was received at the verbal level). (OBS-064-031; OBS-064-032)

31.session_b_note: Contrition itself does not produce the transformation — it is the precondition for transformation. The transformation comes through divine response to contrition (revival — Isa 57:15; salvation — Psa 34:18; acceptance — Psa 51:17). What contrition changes is the person's orientation: from self-sufficiency to disclosed weakness, from merit-claim to open need. The condition (moral condition, suffering) may not change as a result of contrition — Psa 143 presents the enemy still pursuing even as the psalmist is crushed. The transformation is primarily inner-orientational: contrition re-orients the person's posture before God, making them receptive to divine action. Source: OBS-030-046, OBS-030-062, Q&A 015.

Divine forgiveness: triggered by sacrifice/atonement (OT cultic — Lev 4:20), by repentance and faith (NT — Act 2:38, 1Jo 1:9), by intercession (5379-002 corpus — Dan 9:19), and by God's own character-disposition ("to all who call upon you" — Psa 86:5, triggered simply by calling). Human forgiveness: triggered by the offender's repentance (Luk 17:3-4), commanded as an ongoing disposition (Mar 11:25 — in prayer), and grounded in having received forgiveness (the structural logic of Mat 18:35 — the awareness of what one has received triggers the extension). The deepest trigger on the divine side is God's own character — forgiveness is the natural expression of what God is. (OBS-064-019; OBS-064-014; OBS-064-016)

Both are present and distinguishable:
(1) Orientation change — the primary transformation in the experiential group (884-003) is reorientation: Psa 73:28 shows Asaph's inner life transformed not by changed external circumstances but by a reorientation toward God. "It is good to be near God" — the transformation is in where the person now locates goodness.
(2) Character transformation — the Spirit-produced goodness (885-001, 886-002) is a transformation of the person's inner character. The Spirit fills the person with goodness; Col 3:12 images this as putting on a new garment — the character itself changes.
(3) Moral awareness transformation — Eze 36:31 (OBS-035): the transformation from moral blindness to moral self-recognition is an inner condition change — the person who did not see their deeds as not-good comes to see and loathe them. This is a change of condition (from blindness to sight) that precedes orientation change (loathing → turning).

32.session_b_note: All four modes present. Imposes (God's sovereign declaration): "your sins are forgiven" (Luk 7:47-48 — declared without the woman asking for it); "I will forgive their iniquity" (Jer 31:34 — covenantal declaration). Invites (access-postures named): repentance, faith, confession named as access-paths — these invite rather than coerce. Enables: Luk 7:47 shows forgiveness generating love-orientation — the will's disposition is transformed rather than merely redirected. Requires (command): Luk 17:3-4, Mat 18:35 — human forgiveness is commanded; the will is required to act. The relationship with the will is therefore: God's forgiveness can come as declaration (imposed), be received through inner posture (invited), transform the volitional orientation (enabled), and command a corresponding human act (required). (OBS-064-029; OBS-064-031; OBS-064-017)

33.session_b_note: Sequence identified in Q&A 020 and Q&A 038: (1) crushing/breaking — the inner being encounters its moral condition before God; (2) contrite posture — the broken spirit and heart are lowered before God; (3) longing for restoration — Psa 51:8 ("let me hear joy and gladness; let the bones that you have broken rejoice"); (4) divine response — revival/nearness/salvation received. The sequence moves from inner collapse → posture of lowliness → longing → divine reviving. Source: Q&A 015, Q&A 020, OBS-030-055.

Yes — implicitly. The act of forgiveness involves cost for the forgiver: a legitimate claim is cancelled, which means the offended party absorbs the cost of the wrong rather than extracting payment. This is implicit in the debt-cancellation language of the parable (Mat 18:27 — the master "released him and forgave him the debt" — a real financial cost absorbed). The somatic dimension: the blood of Christ (Eph 1:7, Heb 9:22) names the suffering involved in the divine act of forgiving — forgiveness at the highest level is costly to the one who forgives. The OT sacrificial system (5379-001) involves the cost of the sacrificial animal — suffering substituted. The intersection with suffering is not named somatically in the forgiveness-word itself but is embedded in the mechanism (blood, sacrifice, cost-absorption). (OBS-064-022; OBS-064-014)

34.session_b_note: Yes — the outer limit: Mar 3:29 (blasphemy against the Spirit — "never has forgiveness"). Within that limit: the corpus emphasises the breadth rather than the narrowness of forgiveness. "All who call upon you" (Psa 86:5) — bounded only by calling. "Everyone who believes receives forgiveness" (Act 10:43) — bounded only by faith. "All sins will be forgiven the children of man" (Mar 3:28 — the verse immediately preceding Mar 3:29) — explicitly comprehensive before naming the one exception. Luk 7:47 — "her sins, which are many, are forgiven" — the multitude of the sin is not the limit. The structure is: forgiveness is maximally inclusive within the boundary of the Spirit's work; it is exclusively excluded only where the Spirit's work is permanently rejected. (OBS-064-024; Q&A-001 OBS-064-024)

35.session_b_note: Mourning and contrition are closely related but distinguishable. The registry description notes that contrition is "the interior grief of having actually caused harm or broken trust" — which is adjacent to mourning. The verse evidence does not use mourning vocabulary directly in the contrition groups, but the experiential anguish register (Emotion — Negative groups: Psa 143:3, Psa 44:19) represents inner grief/lament. The connection to R151 (sorrow — which shares Isa 53:5 as an anchor) is the strongest data point: in the substitutionary crushing, sorrow and contrition-vocabulary co-occur at the same verse. Mourning may accompany genuine contrition; the data does not establish a causal or sequential relationship. Source: OBS-030-032, OBS-030-041, OBS-030-056.

Primary contrasts: (1) Guilt vs forgiveness — the most direct opposition (51 shared verses with guilt). Guilt is the condition forgiveness addresses; the two are structurally counterpart (OBS-064-011). (2) Judgment vs forgiveness — the parable of Mat 18:35: the servant's forgiveness revoked produces judgment. (3) Remembering vs forgetting — Jer 31:34: "I will remember their sin no more" — divine memory of sin as the opposition to forgiveness's cognitive component. (4) Hardness vs openness — the heart-level requirement (Mat 18:35) sets genuine forgiveness against hardened refusal. (5) Abandonment vs fidelity — Rev 2:4: the wrong use of aphiēmi (abandoning love) contrasted with the right use (releasing guilt). The sharpest defining contrast is guilt vs forgiveness — guilt being the accumulated debt-state that forgiveness cancels.

36.session_b_note: The Spirit is directly present in the forgiveness corpus in two distinct ways. (1) The unforgivable sin: Mar 3:29 — blasphemy against the Holy Spirit as the condition that permanently closes forgiveness. This names the Spirit's role in the forgiveness-possibility: the Spirit is the one whose work creates and sustains the inner openness through which forgiveness is received. Permanent rejection of the Spirit = permanent closure of forgiveness. (2) Joh 20:23 — "if you forgive the sins of any, they are forgiven; if you withhold forgiveness from any, it is withheld." This authority to forgive is given in the context of the Spirit's bestowal (Joh 20:22 — "receive the Holy Spirit"). Forgiveness-authority is Spirit-given. Additionally: the sb_classification names the spirit level as the origin-level of forgiveness (God's spirit-level disposition). (OBS-064-024; OBS-064-002)

Goodness produces a particular quality of inner stability characterised by rootedness in God rather than in circumstances:
- Psa 73:28 (OBS-029): Asaph's inner stability after his crisis of doubt is entirely grounded in nearness to God — "it is good to be near God; I have made the Lord God my refuge." The stability is relational and divine-oriented, not circumstantial.
- The doxological group (884-001): the declaration "for he is good, his steadfast love endures forever" (Psa 100:5, 106:1, 107:1) names an enduring quality — God's goodness as the stable reference point regardless of circumstances. The human person's inner stability is anchored in this enduring divine character.
- Contrasted with Haman (Est 5:9, OBS-040): the inner well-being not grounded in God is instantly disrupted — its instability marks the absence of genuine rootedness.
The quality of stability produced by goodness is: oriented toward God, grounded in his enduring character, resistant to circumstantial disruption.

The inner well-being state of 884-009 (tov-lev, glad of heart, shalom-condition) sits between Emotion — Positive (if the dimension vocabulary includes this) and Vitality/Existence. The group describes an affective inner state that is neither primarily moral, cognitive, nor volitional. The closest current dimension is Emotion — Positive, but if the programme's 10-dimension vocabulary uses 'Vitality / Existence' (as noted in the dimension review instruction), that may be the better fit for well-being states.

**GAP QUESTION — GAP-N-002:**
> For verse context groups that name inner affective well-being states (glad of heart, shalom-condition, prospering inwardly) that are not primarily moral assessments, cognitive evaluations, or volitional acts, should the dimension be Emotion — Positive or Vitality / Existence? The two dimensions need clearer boundary criteria for these borderline cases.

37.session_b_note: The soul-level operation of forgiveness is the exercise-ground named in the sb_classification: the human volitional act of forgiving from the heart. Mat 18:35 — "from your heart" — the heart is the seat of the will and the whole inner person, which in Hebrew thought (lēb) encompasses the soul-level volitional life. The commanded human act of forgiveness (Luk 17:3-4) is a soul-level act — within the human's volitional capacity when commanded. The distinction in the sb_classification: spirit-level = the origin (God's disposition, divine act, transformative capacity); soul-level = the exercise (human volitional decision to release, commanded and achievable). The soul mediates the received forgiveness into extended forgiveness. (OBS-064-002; OBS-064-031)

38.session_b_note: The body's relationship to forgiveness is: (1) Mechanism: the blood of Christ (Eph 1:7, Heb 9:22) — the physical body as the site of the atoning act that enables forgiveness. (2) Evidence: the woman of Luk 7:47 expresses received forgiveness through bodily acts (weeping, anointing) — the body as the surface on which inner transformation becomes visible. (3) Downstream: the paralytic's healing (Mat 9:2-6) follows immediately after forgiveness pronounced — suggesting a connection between inner forgiveness and bodily restoration. (4) The visceral compassion (*splagchnizomai*, Mat 18:27) as precondition of forgiveness — the body's visceral response (gut, spleen) as the felt origin of the act of forgiving. The body is not named as the primary locus of forgiveness, but is present as mechanism, evidence, downstream, and precondition. (OBS-064-030; OBS-064-022)

Three capacities are named:
(1) Instruction — Rom 15:14: "full of goodness, filled with all knowledge, and able to instruct one another." Goodness produces the capacity to teach and build up others in the community.
(2) Kindness as active relational quality — Col 3:12: chrēstotēs as garment of the renewed person enables generous relational disposition toward others. The capacity is dispositional — not occasional kindness but a settled orientation.
(3) Justice and right action — Mic 6:8: the person who knows what is good has the capacity to do justice toward others and to love kindness in relational practice.
(4) Beneficence/generosity — agathōsunē glossed as "generosity" in Gal 5:22 (OBS-008): the capacity to act generously toward others as an expression of Spirit-produced goodness.

39.session_b_note: The registry description explicitly distinguishes contrition from shame: "not the surface shame of being caught but the interior grief of having actually caused harm or broken trust." The contrition vocabulary (dkh/ktt/nkh roots) does not include shame vocabulary. The inner-being distinction is that shame is often oriented toward external observers (one's standing in others' eyes) while contrition is oriented toward the actual harm done and toward God. The verse evidence does not use shame terminology — the contrite posture is consistently before God, not before a social audience. Source: OBS-030-001, OBS-030-003.

All four: (1) Act — the discrete event of pronouncing or releasing forgiveness ("your sins are forgiven" — Luk 7:47-48; Lev 4:20 atonement outcome). (2) State — the condition of being forgiven (afesis — the state of release from guilt; group 879-001). (3) Disposition — readiness-to-forgive as divine character (sal.lach — Psa 86:5). (4) Quality — the abstract possession of forgiveness as a divine attribute (se.li.chah — Psa 130:4; Dan 9:9). The four forms are linguistically distinct (verb/noun/adjective/abstract noun in the Hebrew; verb/noun in the Greek) and analytically distinct. The registry's sb_classification must account for all four: disposition (spirit-level origin) → act (both divine and human) → state (received) → quality (divine possession). (OBS-064-003; OBS-064-019; OBS-064-005)

40.session_b_note: The registry description places contrition as the inner precondition for genuine repentance: "without it, repentance is merely a change of strategy rather than a change of heart." The registry note references repentance (#135) as a related registry. The verse evidence supports this directionally: the crushed/contrite heart is the inner posture before the turn (repentance), not its product. Psa 51:17 (broken and contrite heart as sacrifice) precedes any specific repentance-vocabulary in the psalm; the contrition is the interior state from which repentance arises. Source: OBS-030-001, OBS-030-002.

Forgiveness as the ground of hope: Psa 130:4 — "with you there is forgiveness, that you may be feared" — the Psalm (De Profundis) moves from depths of guilt to hope grounded in the knowledge that forgiveness is available with God. The context of Psa 130 is the Psalmist's cry from the depths, and the discovery that forgiveness belongs to God is the turn toward hope (Psa 130:5-7 — "I wait for the Lord... for with the Lord there is steadfast love, and with him is plentiful redemption"). Jer 31:34 — the new covenant forgiveness as the basis of the future hope of the covenant people. The forgiveness corpus positions forgiveness as the ground of hope for the guilty: because forgiveness is available, guilt is not the final word. (OBS-064-020; OBS-064-017)

41.session_b_note: The verse evidence shows that contrition and joy do not exclude each other; they exist in sequential relationship. Psa 51:8 — "let me hear joy and gladness; let the bones that you have broken rejoice" — presents the psalmist's contrite position as the ground from which joy is desired. The contrite person does not possess joy at the moment of contrition but longs for it and expects it through divine response. The sequence: contrition → longing for joy → divine reviving → joy received. Contrition is therefore the threshold through which joy is sought, not its opposite in a static sense. Source: OBS-030-055, Q&A 040.

Not named directly in the anchor verses. However: Rom 4:7 — "blessed are those whose lawless deeds are forgiven, and whose sins are covered" — makarios (blessed, happy) names a condition of well-being that attaches to being forgiven. This is indirect joy/blessing language. The Luk 7:47 context involves a woman whose devotion is intense and whose state is of profound gratitude — the emotional quality is close to joy though not named as such. The corpus does not provide direct evidence of forgiveness → joy as a named causal chain, unlike the love-chain (Luk 7:47) or the fear-chain (Psa 130:4). (OBS-064-029)

The most direct evidence: Rom 3:12 (OBS-046) — "all have turned aside; together they have become worthless; no one does good, not even one." The inner-being condition of the person without goodness is: turned aside (direction reversed), worthless (character emptied), unable to do good (capacity absent). This is the universal-depravity diagnosis — total inner-being failure at the goodness level.
Eze 36:31 (OBS-035) describes the prior condition of those who have not yet received the transforming recognition: walking in evil ways, doing deeds that are not good, under iniquity and abomination — the condition is morally blind, self-directed, unaware of the verdict on their ways.
Jer 6:16 names the condition of those who refuse the good way as one of deliberate refusal: the way is offered and rejected. This is not ignorance but wilful resistance.

42.session_b_note: Jer 31:34 — the most theologically central verse: it joins divine forgiveness with the new covenant, direct knowledge of God, and cognitive cessation of the guilt-claim ("remember no more"). This verse establishes forgiveness as the covenantal-structural foundation of the new human-divine relationship: forgiveness is not a periodic renewal but a permanent new-covenant condition. The verse's theological centrality is confirmed by its status as shared anchor with three other registries (guilt, love, thought/knowledge). (OBS-064-017; OBS-064-018)

43.session_b_note: Luk 7:47 — highest inner-being density: forgiveness received, love produced, quantity-proportional relationship, implied guilt-state resolved. The verse names: (1) the act of forgiving (aphiēmi perfect passive — already forgiven); (2) the inner state of the recipient (loved much — agapaō, inner orientation); (3) the causal relationship (because forgiven → loved); (4) the comparative case (forgiven little → loves little). Four inner-being elements in one clause. (OBS-064-029; OBS-064-021)

Mic 6:8 implies the relationship without stating it explicitly: God tells the human what is good — the knowledge of goodness flows from God to the human. The registry description (OBS-001) makes the relationship explicit: human goodness is derivative — a participation in and reflection of divine goodness. Psa 119:68 is more direct — "You are good and do good; teach me your statutes" — the human learns goodness from the God who is good. The relationship is: divine goodness is originative and instructive; human goodness is responsive and dependent.

Group 884-007's Gen 1:31 anchor establishes that goodness is first a divine creative-evaluative act — ontological before moral. The group is distinct from all other divine goodness groups (884-001 doxological, 884-005 covenantal) in that it is pre-relational: it precedes any divine-human relationship. The creation pronouncement has no human correlate — no human analogue for declaring creation 'very good' exists. This uniqueness is why no current dimension label fits. Provisional assignment: Dimension 11 (Divine-Human Correspondence) with a note that the dimension review should consider whether this group requires a distinct sub-category.

Gen 1:31 ('God saw everything that he had made, and behold, it was very good') is the anchor for 884-007. The creative pronouncement establishes goodness as an ontological category prior to any human inner-being engagement. The divine evaluative act here is creative-constitutive: God declares creation good and in declaring it so, the goodness is established. This is distinct from God's ongoing disposition toward humanity (884-001 — doxological/relational) and from God's covenantal promise-keeping (884-005). The provisional dimension: Dimension 11 — Divine-Human Correspondence is the closest available label, but the group is distinctive enough to warrant a sub-category or notation in the dimension review.

**GAP QUESTION — GAP-N-003:**
> For verse context groups where God's inner-being engagement is a creative-constitutive evaluative act (declaring creation good, establishing ontological goodness) rather than a relational disposition toward humanity, should Dimension 11 (Divine-Human Correspondence) apply, or should the dimension review introduce a distinct label for this ontological-creative register?

44.session_b_note: Mat 18:35 — "if you do not forgive your brother from your heart." Most clearly shows inner-being operation: the heart-locus requirement distinguishes the inner-being reality of forgiveness from its verbal/legal surface. The verse states that forgiveness is not complete at the outer level — it must reach the inner being (heart) to be genuine. The inner-being operation named: heart-level releasing of the claim. (OBS-064-031)

45.session_b_note: Eph 1:7 — "in him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses, according to the riches of his grace." Shows the full theological depth: grace (upstream source) → blood (atoning mechanism) → redemption + forgiveness (twin outcomes) → "riches of his grace" (quantifying the standard). The verse locates forgiveness within the complete theological architecture: divine character (grace), cosmic event (atonement), and experienced state (forgiveness received). (OBS-064-022; OBS-064-023)

Both G0019 (agathōsunē, 885-001, Dimension 04 — Volition) and G5544 (chrēstotēs, 886-002, Dimension 05 — Moral Character) anchor at Gal 5:22. Their co-presence in the fruit list as adjacent but distinct items names the programme's analytical distinction: agathōsunē is goodness as virtue and resolve (volitional — the Spirit-enabled resolve for good, 2Th 1:11); chrēstotēs is kindness as relational disposition and character quality (moral character — the Spirit-produced inner quality expressed toward others). The co-anchor confirms that Gal 5:22 is a single verse that simultaneously engages two analytically distinct inner-being dimensions of goodness. The dimensions are not in conflict — they are facets of a unified Spirit-produced character.

46.session_b_note: Psa 130:4 — "there is forgiveness with you, that you may be feared." The counterintuitive observation: the knowledge that forgiveness is available produces not presumption but awe. This reverses the expected human response (availability of pardon → relaxing of standards / presumption on mercy). The verse names a second-order inner-being dynamic: the person who genuinely encounters the depth of God's capacity to forgive is moved to fear, not emboldened to sin. The counterintuitive element is the emotional/dispositional direction — fear rather than relief or joy. (OBS-064-020)

47.session_b_note: Luk 17:4 — "if he sins against you seven times in the day, and turns to you seven times, saying 'I repent,' you must forgive him." Most demanding context: repeated forgiveness of repeated offenses within a single day, conditional only on verbal repentance, commanded without limit to the number of repetitions. The verse places forgiveness in the context of ongoing relational cost and repetition.
