The inner-being demand is significant: the willingness to release the claim must be repeatedly exercised without accumulation of grievance. (OBS-064-031 by implication)

Per Q&A-016 (OBS-016 equivalent). Three directions: (1) Divine → human: God's goodness flows toward the human person through character, word, creation, and Spirit. (2) Inward → outward: goodness received in the inner being expresses itself outward in conduct, community, and testimony. (3) Vertical → horizontal: the vertical orientation (walking with God, nearness to God) produces horizontal expression (justice, kindness toward others). The directional structure is: downward from God → inward to the human person → outward to community.

48.session_b_note: Mar 2:7 — "who can forgive sins but God alone?" — the scribes' question is the most direct statement of the divine exclusivity of forgiveness at its deepest level. The verse frames the question that Jesus then resolves: the authority to forgive sins belongs to God; Jesus claims and exercises it; this is the Christological claim embedded in the forgiveness corpus. Divine action and human reception are most clearly distinguished at this verse: forgiveness is not a human negotiation or transaction but a divine act. (OBS-064-003 by implication)

Two connections are present: (1) Lam 3:25–27 (OBS in Q&A-035): in the context of profound suffering (Lamentations), the declaration that "the Lord is good to those who wait for him" situates divine goodness as sustaining and accessible in affliction. Suffering does not negate goodness — it becomes the context in which the goodness of waiting for God is discovered. (2) Psa 34:8 (OBS-025): Psalm 34 is composed in the context of David's distress. The invitation to "taste and see that the Lord is good" is issued from within a crisis. Goodness and affliction are not opposed — affliction is a context in which divine goodness is specifically offered.

49.session_b_note: This is one of the richest connections in the registry. Eze 36:31 (OBS-035) is the primary evidence: the recognition of deeds as not-good produces self-loathing before God — moral failure is the catalyst for the deepest moral awareness. Rom 3:12 (OBS-046) names the universal absence of goodness as the diagnosis of the fallen human condition. Group 884-006 (22 verses) is entirely dedicated to the not-good verdict — moral failure named and assessed. Goodness and moral failure are structurally related: goodness is understood partly through its absence in moral failure; and the recognition of moral failure is the first movement toward goodness. The shared anchor at Eze 36:31 with Registry 1 (abomination) confirms this connection.

50.session_b_note: Group 884-005 ("God's good word and promise — covenantal faithfulness") is the primary evidence. Jos 23:14 (OBS-033): God's good promises are received and known in the "hearts and souls" of the people — covenantal faithfulness is internalised. The repeated formula in Ezra and Nehemiah ("the good hand of my God was upon me" — Ezr 7:9, Neh 2:8) names divine goodness as the operative power behind covenantal provision. God's good word is inherently covenantal — it is the promise made and kept within relationship. The doxological refrain "he is good, his steadfast love endures forever" (Psa 100:5, 106:1 etc.) links goodness directly to hesed (steadfast love) — the primary covenantal disposition of God.

60.session_b_note: Reception — consistently. Col 3:12: identity is established by prior reception — "God's chosen ones, holy and beloved" — this is their standing before the commission follows. Gal 5:22: the Spirit produces the fruit; the believer receives it. The good person in 884-002 is assessed by the quality of character (Pro 12:2 — "a good man obtains favour from the Lord") — but this character is itself a participation in divine goodness (OBS-001), not autonomous achievement. The ground of identity in this registry is reception and relationship, not self-achieved virtue.

65.session_b_note: SBF-VCB013-001 proposed a tripartite model for chrēstotēs: presence (Spirit-produced quality and God's disposition), absence (Rom 3:12), distortion (not strongly attested in verse corpus). This session's analysis extends the model: the distortion pole is etymologically embedded in G5542 (chrēstologia — smooth talk), a related word of G5544. The full registry supports the presence/absence structure: 884-006 (not-good, 22 verses) for H2896A; 886-002/Rom 3:12 for G5544. Distortion: Mal 2:17 (calling evil good), Jer 44:17 (redefining good as material advantage), G5542 (chrēstologia — the etymological distortion form). The tripartite structure is confirmed across the full registry.

69.session_b_note: Deu 9:6 is the primary evidence: "not because of your righteousness, for you are a stubborn people" — the explicit negation of any moral basis. The good land and God's goodness are given despite (not because of) the people's character. Rom 11:22 (OBS-043) presents the condition differently — the recipient has no claim on divine kindness that is independent of God's character; the conditionality named is about continuation, not origination. The absence of any basis is more explicit in the Deuteronomy context than in the NT material for this registry.

70.session_b_note: Hebrew primary: H5545 (sa.lach) — root SALACH. The root does not have a clear concrete physical referent in other Semitic languages — it is primarily a religious-moral term in OT usage, not clearly derived from a physical act (unlike, e.g., nasa = lift/carry, which grounds forgiveness metaphorically in the lifting of a burden). Greek primary: G0863 (afiēmi — root APO + HIEMI = "to send away from"). The compound is transparent: *apo* (away from) + *hiēmi* (to send, let go) = to release, let go away. The root etymology of afiēmi grounds the act physically in the image of releasing something from a hold — letting it go away. This physical image underlies all the semantic senses: the sin-claim is sent away; the family is let go away; the access is permitted (the barrier is sent away). (OBS-064-004; OBS-064-003)

71.session_b_note: Group 884-004's comparative wisdom sayings use tov as a ranking operator that makes structural normative claims about the relative value of inner-being characteristics vs external achievements. Pro 15:16 argument: premise — fear of the Lord is an inner orientation; premise — great treasure with trouble is the external alternative; conclusion — the inner orientation (a little with fear of the Lord) surpasses the external condition. Pro 16:32 argument: premise — slow to anger and self-rule are inner mastery qualities; premise — being mighty and taking a city are peak external achievements; conclusion — inner mastery exceeds external achievement. The logical structure is consistent across the wisdom comparatives: inner quality > outer achievement, because goodness is the evaluative framework that ranks them.

72.session_b_note: Hebrew SALACH family: H5545 (verb — act of forgiving), H5546 (adjective — character of being forgiving), H5547 (noun — forgiveness as abstract quality). The three forms of the same root cover the three aspects: act, disposition, quality. This lexical completeness is analytically significant — the Hebrew vocabulary provides three grammatical forms naming the same reality from three angles. The family is closed — there are no broader SALACH cognates with different meanings (unlike, e.g., RACHAM family which covers both womb and compassion). Greek AFE family: G0859 (afesis — noun, state of release), G0863G/H/I (afiēmi — verb, act of releasing, with three sub-entries by dominant sense). The three sub-entries reflect not three distinct words but one verb used in three dominant modes. (OBS-064-003; OBS-064-004)

73.session_b_note: H5545 sub-sense 1b: "(Niphal) to be forgiven." The Niphal passive form names the received state of forgiveness from the worshipper's perspective — they are forgiven, the act is done to them. This is the dominant form in the OT cultic corpus (Lev 4:20 — "they shall be forgiven"; repeated throughout the Levitical atonement passages). The Niphal grounds the pasivity of the recipient: forgiveness is not self-generated but received as the outcome of an external act. The active Qal form (God forgives) and the Niphal (they are forgiven) together describe the two sides of the same event. (OBS-064-014; OBS-064-005)

74.session_b_note: Key translation losses: (1) H5545 divine-exclusivity is invisible in English "forgive" — English "forgive" is used freely for both human and divine subjects, whereas the Hebrew reader knows sa.lach is exclusively divine. English readers miss the Christological significance of Mar 2:7 because English "forgive" doesn't carry the subject-restriction signal. (2) The aphiēmi/afesis semantic breadth is lost in English: "forgive" captures only G0863H and G0859, while "leave" (G0863G) and "permit" (G0863I) appear as different words. English readers don't see the shared root connecting the discipleship-leaving vocabulary (Mat 10:37) with the forgiveness vocabulary (Luk 7:47). (3) The distinction between aphiēmi (act) and afesis (state) is collapsed in "forgive/forgiveness" — the Greek distinguishes the act from the resulting state more precisely. (4) Sal.lach (adjective: character-attribute) and salah (verb: act) are both translated "forgiving/forgive" — the dispositional vs act distinction is flattened. (OBS-064-003; OBS-064-004; OBS-064-006)

75.session_b_note: The command form appears in: Luk 17:3-4 ("you must forgive him" — repeated); Mar 11:25 ("forgive... so that your Father also may forgive"); Mat 6:12/Luk 11:4 (the prayer petition which implies a practice-command). The command form treats forgiveness as volitionally achievable — the inner act can be commanded because the will has the capacity to perform it (with the proviso of the heart-locus requirement in Mat 18:35). The command also assumes resistance (otherwise it need not be commanded). The command form reveals that forgiveness at the human level is a volitional act, not merely a feeling or a spontaneous response. It can be willed; it is expected to be willed; and its absence is morally culpable. (OBS-064-031; OBS-064-032)

Rom 3:12 (OBS-046) is the primary evidence for universal absence: "no one does good, not even one" — the complete withdrawal/absence of chrēstotēs from fallen humanity reveals that goodness is not a default human capacity but something that requires a source outside the natural human condition. Its absence defines what fallen humanity is. Eze 36:31 (OBS-035): the recognition of deeds as not-good reveals that goodness has a structural role as the evaluative standard — its absence is measured against its presence as the norm. Jer 6:16: the absence of goodness-walking produces restlessness and judgment. The absence reveals goodness as constitutive of human flourishing and covenantal standing.

76.session_b_note: Jer 26:14 (Group 884-008 anchor, OBS-038): Jeremiah's "do with me as seems good and right to you" spoken under threat of death — this is the volitional-preference idiom in its most extreme form. The depth revealed: genuine goodness-orientation involves the capacity to yield one's own life to another's judgment. The inner depth is one of radical volitional submission. Lam 3:25–27 in extremity (national devastation, personal grief): "it is good to wait quietly for the salvation of the Lord" — goodness at its deepest is the capacity to orient toward God even in the absence of all external support.

77.session_b_note: Group 884-006 (not-good verdict) provides the primary judicial evidence. Eze 36:31 (OBS-035): goodness functions as the standard against which conduct is judicially assessed — deeds are measured as not-good against the norm. Eze 18:18: "he did what is not good among his people, behold he shall die for his iniquity" — the not-good verdict carries judicial consequence (death). In the judicial context, goodness functions as the standard of moral evaluation — it defines what passes and what fails the test.

Group 884-007's Gen 1:31 anchor establishes that goodness is first a divine creative-evaluative act — ontological before moral. The group is distinct from all other divine goodness groups (884-001 doxological, 884-005 covenantal) in that it is pre-relational: it precedes any divine-human relationship. The creation pronouncement has no human correlate — no human analogue for declaring creation 'very good' exists. This uniqueness is why no current dimension label fits. Provisional assignment: Dimension 11 (Divine-Human Correspondence) with a note that the dimension review should consider whether this group requires a distinct sub-category.

86.session_b_note: The verse evidence confirms connections to: (1) Guilt (073) — primary structural counterpart (51 shared verses; guilt is the state forgiveness addresses). (2) Mercy/hesed (111) — Psa 86:5 apposition; Lev 4:20 shared anchor; forgiveness flows from mercy. (3) Repentance (135) — 12 shared verses; repentance as the human access-posture for forgiveness. (4) Faith (059) — 9 shared verses; faith as the NT access-instrument. (5) Love (103) — Luk 7:47; Jer 31:34 shared anchor; love as downstream state. (6) Authority (197) — Mar 2:7; the divine prerogative claim. (7) Spirit (184) — Mar 3:29, Joh 20:23; Spirit's role in forgiveness-possibility. (8) Reconciliation (130) — Mat 5:24 shared anchor. (9) Grace (068) — Eph 1:7; grace as upstream source. (10) Compassion (023) — Mat 18:27; visceral compassion as precondition. (OBS-064-011; OBS-064-012)

87.session_b_note: One new SD pointer raised in this session: SP-064-001 (Mat 5:24 worship-ordering principle). The pre-existing 22 SD pointers comprehensively cover the major cross-registry questions. No additional SD pointers required beyond SP-064-001. (OBS-064-028; OBS-064-018; OBS-064-012)

88.session_b_note: C17 cluster companions include mercy (111), compassion (023), love (103), grace (068), and reconciliation (130) among others. Mercy — Psa 86:5 places sal.lach in apposition with hesed (mercy); mercy is the co-attribute from which forgiveness flows. Compassion — Mat 18:27 shows splagchnizomai (visceral compassion) as the felt inner precondition of the forgiving act. Love — Luk 7:47: love is the primary named downstream state of received forgiveness. Grace — Eph 1:7: grace is the upstream source-disposition from which forgiveness flows. Reconciliation — Mat 5:24 shared anchor: forgiveness enables the relational restoration that reconciliation names. The C17 relationship is hierarchical: grace (upstream disposition) → forgiveness (specific relational act) → love and reverence (downstream states). (OBS-064-019; OBS-064-022; OBS-064-029; Q&A-003)

The 10 sub-senses of H2896A (pleasant/agreeable to senses 1a; pleasant to higher nature 1b; good/excellent of kind 1c; good/rich/valuable 1d; good/appropriate/becoming 1e; better comparative 1f; glad/happy/prosperous 1g; good understanding intellectual 1h; good/kind/benign 1i; good/right ethical 1j) map directly onto the nine verse context groups — the lexical breadth is real semantic range, not translational artefact. The conceptual origin of tov is phenomenological: beginning with experienced pleasantness, rising to moral and covenantal categories.

89.session_b_note: Guilt (073) is the primary structural counterpart — 51 shared verses, the strongest co-occurrence signal. Guilt names the accumulated debt-state, the legitimate claim registered against the person. Forgiveness cancels the claim. Inner-being sequence: sin → guilt (registered moral debt) → forgiveness (cancellation of the debt) → release from guilt-state. The two registries together describe the full cycle. Jer 31:34 (shared anchor) names both simultaneously: "I will forgive their iniquity" → "I will remember their sin no more." (OBS-064-018; OBS-064-011)

Two Greek OWNER terms compete for primacy. G0019 (agathōsunē — goodness) is the more directly named ("goodness") but has only 4 verses. G5544 (chrēstotēs — kindness) has 7 verses across 2 groups. G0019's range: goodness, virtue, beneficence, generosity — it spans inner character (virtue), outward benefit (beneficence), and practical generosity. G5544's range: kindness, goodness, gentleness — it spans relational disposition (kindness), inner quality (goodness), and the divine attribute (generous goodwill). Both terms reveal that Greek goodness vocabulary is more specialised than H2896A — each term occupies a narrower semantic range, producing the NT's precision about the specific form goodness takes.
Source: Section D · OBS-008, OBS-009

90.session_b_note: The existing 22 SD pointers comprehensively cover the major Session D questions. New pointer SP-064-001 adds the worship-ordering principle. Highest-priority existing: DIM-064-SD011 (guilt→forgiveness→love chain), DIM-064-SD017 (grace as source), DIM-064-SD004 (Jer 31:34 three-registry convergence), DIM-064-SD016 (Psa 86:5 cluster). No additional Session D questions identified. (OBS-064-011; OBS-064-012; SP-064-001)

96.session_b_note: G5544 (chrēstotēs) carries both the divine expression (886-001: God's inner disposition of generous goodwill, Dimension 11 — Divine-Human Correspondence) and the human expression (886-002: Spirit-produced inner quality of the believer, Dimension 05 — Moral Character) with the same lexical term. The bipartite split is structurally coherent and consistent with H2896A's same pattern (884-001 Divine-Human Correspondence; 884-002 Moral Character). The programme applies Dimension 11 to divine-subject goodness groups and Dimension 05 to human-subject goodness groups consistently across both OWNER terms. This is a programme-wide structural principle, not a coincidence of this registry.

Both G0019 (agathōsunē, 885-001, Dimension 04 — Volition) and G5544 (chrēstotēs, 886-002, Dimension 05 — Moral Character) anchor at Gal 5:22. Their co-presence in the fruit list as adjacent but distinct items names the programme's analytical distinction: agathōsunē is goodness as virtue and resolve (volitional — the Spirit-enabled resolve for good, 2Th 1:11); chrēstotēs is kindness as relational disposition and character quality (moral character — the Spirit-produced inner quality expressed toward others). The co-anchor confirms that Gal 5:22 is a single verse that simultaneously engages two analytically distinct inner-being dimensions of goodness. The dimensions are not in conflict — they are facets of a unified Spirit-produced character.

97.session_b_note:
The LXX connection is not directly addressed in the Stage 2a observations. What can be inferred: G5544 (chrēstotēs) in its meaning parse (Section D) references Rom 3:12 which is a quotation from Ps 14:3 LXX — confirming that chrēstotēs was used in the LXX to render a Hebrew moral-quality term. The related words G5541 (chrēsteuomai — be kind) and G5543 (chrēstos — good/kind) suggest a LXX-rooted word family that the NT inherits. For H2896A, the LXX rendering as agathos/kalos provides the lexical bridge to NT usage. This is noted as thin evidence — LSJ data was absent for G0019 and G5544 (Section D notes).
Source: Section D · OBS-009

109.session_b_note:
G5544 (chrēstotēs) may carry this. In classical Greek, chrēstos (related form) referred broadly to "useful, serviceable, good" — applicable to persons, tools, or conditions. In the NT, chrēstotēs is spiritually and pneumatologically specialised: it becomes a Spirit-fruit (Gal 5:22) and a divine attribute (Rom 11:22, Eph 2:7). The direction shifts from horizontal utility (what is serviceable) to vertical character (what the Spirit produces and what God embodies). This is not a reversal of direction so much as a concentration and elevation of meaning. LSJ data absent — this observation is partially supported only.
Source: Section D · OBS-009

122.session_b_note: The co-occurrence top three: R103 (love, 23 shared verses) is the dominant connection — confirmed at root level and at the shared anchor Mic 6:8 and Gal 5:22. R43 (desire, 18 shared verses) is the second — the desire vocabulary overlaps with H2896A's volitional-preference and experiential-good groups in the Hebrew wisdom and Psalms literature where what one desires (H183 avah, H2530 chamad) co-appears with what is good (tov). R197 (authority, 17 shared verses) is third but is driven by formulaic co-occurrence in the volitional-preference idiom (884-008) in royal/narrative contexts rather than conceptual connection — the 'do what seems good to you' formula appears in authority contexts, producing structural co-occurrence without semantic connection.

124.session_b_note: XREF sharing structure: R103 (love) shares both H2896A and G0019 — the deepest structural link (2 shared strongs). R42 (delight) shares H2896A only — Hebrew-side connection, suggesting delight and tov overlap specifically in OT wisdom and Psalms literature. R99 (kindness) shares G5544 — Greek-side connection, the expected overlap between chrēstotēs (kindness) and a kindness registry. The tripartite connection pattern (love deepest, delight Hebrew-only, kindness Greek-only) reflects the programme's semantic distribution: the TOV root connects to love and delight in the Hebrew domain; the chrēsto root connects to kindness in the Greek domain.

126.session_b_note:
Confirmed dimensions in R67 (from Section F): Moral Character (05) — 4 groups; Cognition (03) — 2 groups; Volition (04) — 1 group; Divine-Human Correspondence (11) — 2 groups; NULL — 3 groups. Each of these dimensions is almost certainly shared with adjacent characteristics — Moral Character (05) is likely the most common dimension across C10 and related clusters. Whether any single cluster shares all confirmed dimensions simultaneously is not determinable from Stage 2a data alone — this requires cross-cluster analysis (Session D territory).
Source: Section F · OBS-012

130.session_b_note: 884-008 (40 verses, volitional preference idiom, anchor Jer 26:14) is the largest group and is squarely volitional. The group description names choosing, preferring, agreeing, judging fitting — all volitional operations. Goodness reconstitutes the will: it is both the predicate of volitional choice ('what seems good to you') and the character quality that transforms the will's default orientation. Dimension 04 — Volition is the correct assignment. This is confirmed by: (1) the group description; (2) the anchor verse Jer 26:14 (willed submission); (3) the parallel with 885-001 which also carries Volition (04) for Spirit-produced goodness as resolve. Two groups in this registry carry Volition — both represent genuine volitional engagement with goodness.

Already resolved in §N Resolution 008 (OBS-067-OBS-014). OBS-067-OBS-039 is the second observation making the same analytical point. The conclusion is confirmed: Dimension 04 — Volition is the correct assignment for Group 884-008. The group description ('what they choose, prefer, agree to, or judge fitting') is unambiguously volitional. Two groups in R67 carry Volition (04): 884-008 (Hebrew volitional-preference idiom) and 885-001 (Greek Spirit-produced goodness as resolve) — both represent genuine volitional dimensions of goodness.

133.session_b_note:
The shared anchor at Psa 34:8 with Registry 59 (faith — per Section G.3) is the structural connection point. Faith and goodness are structurally related at the moment of encounter: "taste and see that the Lord is good; blessed is the man who takes refuge in him" — the act of taking refuge is a faith-act produced by the encounter with divine goodness. Goodness is thus prior to faith in sequence (encounter with goodness → faith response); faith is the inner-being response to tasting goodness. The distinction: goodness is the character attribute encountered; faith is the inner-being response to the encounter. 2Th 1:11 connects them from the other direction: God completes "every resolve for good and every work of faith" — goodness and faith are co-products of divine enabling, parallel expressions.
Source: Section G.3, Section J · OBS-025

138.session_b_note: Mic 6:8 co-anchors with: compassion (R23), condemnation (R24), humility (R80), kindness (R99), love (R103), will (R173) — six registries. Gal 5:22 co-anchors with: faith (R59), joy (R97), love (R103), patience (R116), peace (R117) — five registries. Both verses function as programme-defined cross-registry synthesis nodes where multiple inner-being characteristics converge in a single text. Mic 6:8 is the OT node defining human moral goodness as a tri-fold orientation (justice, loving kindness, humble walk). Gal 5:22 is the NT node defining Spirit-produced inner-being character as a nine-quality cluster. Both anchor verses are primary candidates for Session D synthesis work.

148.session_b_note: Yes. Mar 3:29 names the unforgivable sin — blasphemy against the Holy Spirit — as the condition under which forgiveness is permanently withheld. "Never has forgiveness, but is guilty of an eternal sin." This outer limit reveals that forgiveness is not an unconditional universal — it has a structural boundary condition. The nature of the forgiveness word is revealed by its limit: it is a relational act that requires a relational condition (openness to the Spirit's work) to operate. The permanent closure of that condition produces permanent non-forgiveness. (OBS-064-024)

149.session_b_note: Yes. The aphiēmi act-structure can produce wrong results in two ways: (1) Rev 2:4 — *aphiēmi* applied to love names wrongful abandonment: the same releasing act that is right when releasing a debt-claim is wrong when releasing a relational bond. The church "abandoned" (aphiēmi) first love — using the release-act wrongly. (2) The structural congruence principle in Mat 18:35 implies that forgiving externally/verbally without forgiving "from the heart" is a misuse of the forgiveness act — the outer form without the inner reality. (OBS-064-027; OBS-064-031)

150.session_b_note: Yes. Two verses establish this directly. Mat 6:14-15 states the reciprocal structure: the one who forgives others is forgiven by the Father; the one who does not forgive others is not forgiven. Mat 18:35 (parable of unforgiving servant): the servant who received enormous forgiveness and refused to extend it had his forgiveness revoked. Luk 7:47 adds the causal chain: forgiveness received generates love-orientation, which motivates horizontal relational generosity. The structural pattern: vertical reception (forgiveness from God) → inner transformation (love-orientation produced) → horizontal extension (forgiveness extended to others). The interdependence is not merely ethical instruction but inner-being structural: the received state enables and obligates the extended state. (OBS-064-029; OBS-064-031; OBS-064-032)

151.session_b_note: Yes — subject-restriction is the sharpest structural feature of this registry. H5545 (sa.lach): exclusively divine subject in all 45 OT occurrences. H5546, H5547: divine-referential only. G0863H (afiēmi — forgive): primarily divine subject (Jesus declaring forgiveness, 26 verses in group 5377-001) but also commanded to humans (Luk 17:3-4; Mat 18:21; Mar 11:25 — 11 verses in group 5377-002). G0859 (afesis): used with both divine and human agency. G0863G and G0863I: human subjects primarily. The subject-restriction is most absolute in the Hebrew salach-root: no human ever forgives with salach in the OT. In the NT, the same lexeme (aphiēmi) is used for both divine and human forgiveness. (OBS-064-003; OBS-064-006)

152.session_b_note: Multiple mechanisms are named across the corpus. OT: priestly mediation + sacrifice → forgiveness (Lev 4:20 — entirely external mechanism, inner state of worshipper not specified). NT: (1) Christ's atoning blood as the mechanism: Eph 1:7 ("in him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses"); (2) Confession as the access-act: 1Jo 1:9 ("if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive"); (3) Repentance + baptism: Act 2:38; (4) Faith: Act 10:43 ("everyone who believes receives forgiveness"). The outward-inner relationship: the outer mechanism (sacrifice, confession, faith) is the access-point; the inner reality (guilt released, standing restored) is the effect. The NT consistently names inner-being prerequisites (faith, repentance, confession) where the OT named only outward mechanism (sacrifice). (OBS-064-014; OBS-064-022)

153.session_b_note: Both modes are attested. Unconditional: Jer 31:34 (new covenant declaration — no condition stated, purely divine proclamation), Psa 86:5 (God is forgiving to "all who call upon you" — the condition is calling, not merit). Conditional: NT consistently pairs forgiveness with repentance (Act 2:38, Luk 24:47), faith (Act 10:43), confession (1Jo 1:9), and prayer (Mar 11:25). The conditions are not merit-conditions but access-conditions — they name the inner posture through which forgiveness is received, not the basis on which it is earned. The unconditional character of forgiveness is its basis in God's own nature (Psa 86:5 — sal.lach as intrinsic attribute); the conditionality is in the access-structure for receiving it. (OBS-064-019; OBS-064-016)

154.session_b_note: Compound. Eph 1:7 pairs redemption and forgiveness as two aspects of the same event — suggesting at minimum two components (release from bondage + release from guilt). Jer 31:34 names a third component in the new covenant form: cognitive cessation ("remember no more") as a distinct element of forgiveness beyond the act of pardoning itself. The OT cultic form has: sin identified → sacrifice offered → atonement made → forgiveness declared (a four-stage sequence). The components are not always simultaneous: in the intercessory corpus (group 5379-002), seeking forgiveness and receiving it are two distinct acts (petition → divine response). In the NT, the pronouncement of forgiveness can precede or follow inner transformation (Luk 7:47 — the woman was already forgiven and the love was the evidence). (OBS-064-017; OBS-064-023)

155.session_b_note: Forgiveness makes possible: (1) Restored relational standing before God — "your sins are forgiven" restores what sin had broken (group 5377-001, Luk 7:47-48); (2) Continued relationship despite ongoing failure — Luk 17:3-4 (forgive seven times a day if the person repents); (3) Renewed worship — Mat 5:24 (reconciliation enables the gift to be offered); (4) The inner capacity to love — Luk 7:47 (forgiveness received generates love-orientation). Relational cycle broken: the debt-accountability cycle. Without forgiveness, every wrong creates an ongoing claim that closes relational space — the debt accumulates, the relationship becomes impossible under its weight. Forgiveness cancels the claim and re-opens the relational space. Mat 18:35 (parable) illustrates the cycle: accumulating claims → impossible relationship → either forgiveness-release or judgment. (OBS-064-029; OBS-064-032; OBS-064-028)

156.session_b_note: Yes — multiple dependent acts named. (1) Worship: Mat 5:24 commands leaving the offering to be reconciled first — forgiveness/reconciliation is a prerequisite for right worship. (2) Love: Luk 7:47 names love as the inner state forgiveness generates — forgiveness is the enabling condition for the love-orientation. (3) Knowledge of God: Jer 31:34 — forgiveness of iniquity is the basis on which direct knowledge of God becomes possible ("they shall all know me... for I will forgive their iniquity"). (4) Reverence: Psa 130:4 — the presence of forgiveness enables the proper inner posture of reverence toward God. (OBS-064-017; OBS-064-029; OBS-064-020; OBS-064-028)

157.session_b_note: Yes — Luk 7:47 states this explicitly and proportionally: "she loved much because she was forgiven much; he who is forgiven little, loves little." The proportion is direct — the magnitude of forgiveness received determines the magnitude of love produced. This is the only proportional statement in the corpus about forgiveness and its downstream effects. No other verse explicitly states a proportional relationship between forgiveness magnitude and downstream inner state. (OBS-064-029)

158.session_b_note: The forgiveness registry shares vocabulary with multiple adjacent registries but occupies a relatively distinct lexical space in its primary Hebrew term. H5545 (sa.lach) has no direct cognates outside the SALACH root family — no overlapping vocabulary with guilt, mercy, or love registries at the term level. However, the Greek aphiēmi/afesis vocabulary is extensively shared with the "leave/abandon" semantic domain, producing the G0863G/H/I split. The shared vocabulary primarily exists at the level of co-occurrence (51 shared verses with guilt; 15 with mercy) rather than lexical overlap. The registry's vocabulary is relatively isolated lexically (especially the Hebrew root) but densely connected through verse co-occurrence. This suggests forgiveness names a distinct inner-being act rather than sharing its act-vocabulary with adjacent characteristics — but the act consistently occurs alongside adjacent characteristics in the same verses. (OBS-064-003; OBS-064-011)

159.session_b_note: Yes. H5546 (sal.lach — adjectival, Psa 86:5) and H5547 (se.li.chah — abstract noun, Psa 130:4, Dan 9:9) name forgiveness as a divine attribute and possession. Psa 86:5: "you are... forgiving" — a character attribute. Psa 130:4: "with you there is forgiveness" — a possessed quality. Dan 9:9: "to the Lord belong mercy and forgiveness." These formulations distinguish the divine character-level from the act-level: God is forgiving (attribute) and possesses forgiveness (quality) — distinct from the individual acts of forgiving. The implication for access: because forgiveness belongs to God's character, it is reliably available to those who come to him (Psa 86:5 adds "to all who call upon you"). Access is grounded not in a decision God must make fresh each time but in what he is and what he has. (OBS-064-019; OBS-064-020)

160.session_b_note: The corpus addresses this indirectly. Mar 11:25: "whenever you stand praying, forgive, if you have anything against anyone" — prayer is the context in which forgiveness is exercised (forgiveness embedded in prayer practice). Luk 11:4: "forgive us our sins, for we ourselves forgive everyone who is indebted to us" — ongoing forgiveness of others as a daily practice embedded in the prayer life. The Lord's Prayer frames horizontal forgiveness as a regular discipline rather than a one-time act. Beyond these, the corpus does not name practices that build or sustain forgiveness capacity directly. The structural logic of Luk 7:47 implies that sustained encounter with divine forgiveness received is the primary sustainer (the larger the experienced forgiveness, the larger the love-orientation it generates), but the corpus does not spell this out as a discipline. (OBS-064-029; OBS-064-032)

161.session_b_note: Transitional. The evidence: (1) Luk 7:47 — forgiveness received → love produced (forgiveness as generator of a further state). (2) Psa 130:4 — forgiveness present → reverence produced. (3) Jer 31:34 — forgiveness declared → direct knowledge of God made possible. (4) Act 26:18 — forgiveness received as part of a conversion sequence (blindness → sight → turning → forgiveness → sanctification). (5) Mat 5:24 — forgiveness/reconciliation enables worship. In each case, forgiveness is not a resting-point but a transition that produces or enables a further inner state. The terminal state in the corpus appears to be the downstream product of forgiveness (love, reverence, knowledge) rather than forgiveness itself. However, forgiveness received also produces a stable resting-state: the guilt-state is resolved and the relational standing restored — this is a stable condition, not merely transitional. The answer is therefore: forgiveness is both — it produces a stable relational condition (guilt resolved, standing restored) AND characteristically generates further inner states from that stable condition. (OBS-064-020; OBS-064-029; OBS-064-017)

207.session_b_note: The registry contains three distinct semantic modes under one root (aphiēmi): releasing-as-forgiveness, releasing-as-leaving, releasing-as-permitting. The verse evidence reveals a unified inner logic: all three modes share the same act-structure — the releasing of a hold. In forgiveness, the hold released is the claim against the offender. In leaving, the hold released is attachment to possessions, family, or prior obligations. In permitting, the hold released is the barrier of obstruction or control. The unifying logic is: *aphiēmi* is always the act of no longer holding — whether the thing held is a debt, a relationship, a place, or an access-barrier. The inner-being register shifts across the modes, but the act-structure remains constant. (OBS-064-004; OBS-064-026; OBS-064-027)

208.session_b_note: Yes. The negative form (not forgiving, withholding forgiveness) is attested and engages the same inner-being faculty. Mar 3:29 — "never has forgiveness" — the permanent absence state. Mat 6:15 — "if you do not forgive others... neither will your Father forgive you" — the structural consequence of withholding. Mat 18:35 — the unforgiving servant: the negative form is the refusal to release. Rev 2:4 — abandonment (negative aphiēmi: releasing what should be held). The negative form operates at the same inner locus (the relational will, the heart) but produces closure rather than opening. The inner-being faculty engaged is identical: it is the same relational will that releases in forgiveness that refuses to release in non-forgiveness. (OBS-064-024; OBS-064-032; OBS-064-027)

209.session_b_note: R030 has an implicit negative register: the un-contrite posture (hardened heart, refusal to humble, Jer 44:10 in Group 7552-003). The negative register reveals that contrition requires the inner being to be permeable — able to receive the weight of its own moral condition. The hardened heart (Jer 44:10 — "they have not humbled themselves, nor have they feared, nor walked in my law") is closed to this recognition. The mechanisms revealed: contrition requires (1) inner permeability/openness to divine assessment; (2) fear/reverence as the cognitive-relational companion; (3) alignment with God's word (walking in the law). Absence of these produces the hardened alternative. Source: OBS-030-028, OBS-030-047, Q&A 005.

null_finding_links.session_b_note: The word produces three relational orientations toward others: doing justice (right conduct in community), loving kindness (relational disposition of generous goodwill), and humble walk with God (vertical orientation that grounds the horizontal). Spirit-produced goodness additionally produces the capacity for mutual instruction (Rom 15:14 — 'able to instruct one another') and beneficent action toward others (agathōsunē glossed as generosity in Gal 5:22). Anchor: Mic 6:8 (OBS-027), Rom 15:14.

In God: goodness is located in his character, name, steadfast love, and Spirit (Group 884-001). Walking and way language (Mic 6:8, Jer 6:16) locates goodness relationally in conduct and direction of life. No single somatic organ is exclusively named — the heart is the most frequent site.

'Near' (Psa 73:28), 'refuge' (Psa 34:8), 'walk' and 'way' (Mic 6:8, Jer 6:16). Directional metaphor (path, way) is more prominent than enclosure for this word, consistent with the moral character dimension. Partial because the spatial language is group-specific rather than registry-wide.

H2895 (tov verbal — be pleasing) is assigned to R103 (love) as XREF, not R67. This reveals the programme's structural decision: the verbal mode of tov (active doing, being pleasing to someone) belongs to the love and generosity domains; R67 retains the adjectival/quality sense. Goodness is conceptualised in this registry as a character state, not primarily as an action. The Greek OWNER terms (agathōsunē, chrēstotēs) are abstract nouns — confirming the dispositional/character orientation of this registry.

No spatial or architectural term is present in the vocabulary inventory. The spatial language in the corpus (near, refuge, way) is metaphorical and embedded in verse usage, not in the vocabulary terms themselves. Goodness does not have an associated sacred space or architectural form in its vocabulary.

No supplication term is present in the vocabulary inventory. Goodness is not typically the object of a specific petitionary vocabulary — one does not "entreat" goodness in the way one might entreat grace or mercy. The seeking posture for goodness is more orientation (Psa 73:28 — making God one's refuge) than petition. The absence of a supplication term is itself significant: goodness is not primarily sought through entreaty but through orientation and relationship.

No atonement or propitiation vocabulary is present in this registry. Goodness does not require atonement for its operation — it is a character quality, not a conditional release from penalty. The closest structural condition in the corpus is the Spirit's enabling (Gal 5:22) and covenantal relationship (Jos 23:14), neither of which involves atonement.

G5544 (chrēstotēs) shows concentration and elevation rather than reversal: classical Greek chrēstos meant broadly 'useful, serviceable, good' (applicable to persons and things); in the NT it becomes pneumatologically specialised — Spirit-fruit (Gal 5:22) and divine attribute (Rom 11:22). The direction shifts from horizontal utility to vertical character, a development not a reversal. LSJ absent — partial confidence.
